unity.title: C# and Editor Fundamentals
unity.description: This layer introduces the Unity editor interface and the C# language basics needed to script behavior. Mastering these foundations is essential before touching any game systems.

resources.label: Brackeys How to Make a Game

unity.challenge: Build a scene with a player cube that moves with keyboard input.

children.title: Unity Editor Layout
children.description: The Unity editor is divided into the Scene, Game, Hierarchy, Project, and Inspector panels. Misunderstanding which panel controls what leads to editing the wrong object or losing work when panels are accidentally undocked or reset.

children.title: Prefabs and the Inspector
children.description: Prefabs are reusable GameObject templates that propagate changes to all instances. Without understanding prefab overrides and variants, teams introduce inconsistencies that are nearly impossible to track down in large scenes.

children.title: MonoBehaviour Lifecycle
children.description: Unity calls Awake, Start, Update, and other methods in a specific order during a frame. Calling code in the wrong lifecycle method causes null reference errors, race conditions between components, and inconsistent initialization.

resources.label: Order of Execution for Event Functions

children.title: C# Variables and Data Types
children.description: C# is a strongly typed language where confusing value types (int, float, struct) with reference types (class, array) leads to subtle bugs, unintended copies, and unexpected garbage collection pauses in gameplay.

children.title: GameObjects and Components
children.description: Unity follows a component-entity model where every object is a container of components that each add a single responsibility. Putting too much logic into one MonoBehaviour creates unmaintainable God Objects that break when the project grows.

children.title: Scenes and the Hierarchy
children.description: Scenes define the world loaded at runtime and the hierarchy panel reflects parent-child transform relationships. Improper scene organization makes collaborative work painful and causes unexpected transform offsets when reparenting objects.

children.title: Unity Learn Pathways
children.description: Unity Learn provides guided pathways that introduce the editor and scripting through hands-on projects. Skipping structured learning leads to gaps in fundamentals that become compounding problems when building real games.

children.title: Visual Studio Integration
children.description: Unity integrates tightly with Visual Studio for debugging, IntelliSense, and breakpoints inside Play mode. Without proper IDE setup, developers miss warnings and spend hours on bugs that would surface immediately with proper tooling.

unity.title: Input and Movement
unity.description: This layer covers capturing player input and translating it into smooth character movement. Solid input handling is the backbone of any responsive game.
topics: Legacy input versus new Input System
unity.challenge: Add gamepad and keyboard support to the player with a follow camera.

children.title: New Input System vs Legacy
children.description: The new Input System package uses action maps and bindings decoupled from code, while the legacy system uses string-based axis names. Sticking with legacy input means missing multi-device support, rebinding, and a cleaner architecture needed for modern games.

children.title: Action Maps and Bindings
children.description: Action maps group related inputs (UI, Gameplay, Vehicle) that can be enabled and disabled at runtime. Without mastering action maps, switching control contexts — such as entering a menu — requires fragile manual boolean flags.

children.title: Frame Rate Independent Movement
children.description: Multiplying velocity by Time.deltaTime ensures movement speed is the same on a 30 FPS machine as on a 144 FPS machine. Forgetting deltaTime causes gameplay that runs twice as fast on a higher refresh rate monitor, which is unacceptable in shipped games.

children.title: Transform Manipulation
children.description: Moving objects via Transform.Translate or setting position directly bypasses physics and can cause clipping or jitter. Understanding when to use Transform versus Rigidbody.MovePosition is critical for smooth and collision-aware movement.

children.title: Camera Follow Behavior
children.description: A follow camera must update in LateUpdate after the player moves to avoid one-frame-behind jitter. Implementing camera logic in Update or the wrong script order produces visible stuttering that harms the player experience.

children.title: Gamepad and Keyboard Support
children.description: Supporting multiple device types simultaneously requires the Input System to merge bindings rather than branch on device type. Missing this means code duplication and a brittle system that breaks when adding a new controller.

children.title: Input Readers and Decoupling
children.description: Centralizing input reading into a dedicated component or ScriptableObject event keeps gameplay scripts unaware of device specifics. Tightly coupling input to movement scripts makes it impossible to replay input or swap control schemes cleanly.

children.title: Input Debugging
children.description: The Input Debugger window shows all connected devices and their live values, which is essential when diagnosing why a binding does not fire. Without it, developers waste time in code when the issue is a misconfigured binding or a device not being recognized.

unity.title: Physics and Collisions
unity.description: This layer explores the built-in physics engine for rigidbodies, colliders, and triggers. Understanding physics lets you create believable interactions and detection.
unity.challenge: Add jumping, gravity, and collectible pickups using triggers to the player.

children.title: Rigidbody Physics Modes
children.description: Unity's Rigidbody component has Dynamic, Kinematic, and Static modes that determine whether the physics engine or code controls movement. Mixing up modes causes objects to pass through floors, ignore forces, or produce wildly incorrect collision responses.

children.title: FixedUpdate and Physics Timing
children.description: Physics simulation runs on a fixed timestep separate from the render framerate, so physics code must live in FixedUpdate. Applying forces or moving Rigidbodies in Update creates inconsistent simulation results that vary with frame rate.

children.title: Colliders and Triggers
children.description: A Collider with IsTrigger checked generates overlap callbacks instead of physical responses. Confusing triggers with regular colliders means gameplay events such as pickups or damage zones either fire twice, never fire, or stop objects from moving.

children.title: Layer Collision Matrix
children.description: The Physics Layer Collision Matrix defines which layers can collide with each other, allowing fine-grained filtering without code. Not using layers forces developers to write fragile tag checks inside OnCollisionEnter, which bloats components and introduces bugs when new layers are added.

children.title: Raycasting and Overlap Queries
children.description: Raycasts and overlap checks let code ask spatial questions — is there a wall here, is the player grounded — without requiring physical contact. Relying on collision callbacks for these checks creates a frame of lag and introduces dependency on event ordering.

children.title: Physics Materials and Friction
children.description: Physic Materials set friction and bounciness properties on collider surfaces without writing a single line of code. Neglecting them means ice levels, bouncy balls, and sticky walls all require custom scripts that duplicate what the physics engine already handles.

children.title: 2D Physics Components
children.description: Unity has completely separate physics systems for 2D (Rigidbody2D, Collider2D) and 3D. Accidentally mixing them — attaching a 3D Rigidbody to a 2D sprite — silently produces no physical response, which is a common source of confusion for beginners.

children.title: Collision Callbacks Reference
children.description: OnCollisionEnter, OnTriggerEnter and their Stay and Exit variants follow strict rules about which Rigidbody modes trigger them. Misunderstanding the callback matrix leads to detectors that fire intermittently or not at all.

unity.title: UI and Game State
unity.description: This layer teaches building menus, HUDs, and managing game state across scenes. Clear UI and state flow turn mechanics into an actual playable game.
unity.challenge: Add a main menu, score HUD, and game over screen to the project.

children.title: Canvas and Anchoring
children.description: A UI Canvas renders in Screen Space Overlay, Screen Space Camera, or World Space modes, each suited to different use cases. Getting anchors and pivots wrong causes HUD elements to misalign on different screen resolutions and aspect ratios, which is immediately visible to players.

children.title: UI Toolkit vs uGUI
children.description: Unity now offers two UI systems: the legacy uGUI (Canvas-based) and the newer UI Toolkit with UXML and USS. Choosing the wrong system for a target platform — UI Toolkit lacks runtime support on some mobile builds — causes costly late rewrites.

children.title: Singleton Game Managers
children.description: A GameManager singleton acts as the global authority for score, lives, and game state transitions. Without a clear singleton pattern, multiple scripts hold conflicting state that diverges over time, causing score resets and incorrect game-over triggers.

children.title: Scene Loading and Transitions
children.description: SceneManager.LoadSceneAsync loads scenes without freezing the game, while additive loading enables persistent managers across scenes. Synchronous scene loads cause frame hitches, and not using DontDestroyOnLoad loses manager state between scene switches.

children.title: Score and Health Displays
children.description: UI elements must subscribe to game events or poll a data source rather than receiving direct method calls from gameplay code. Tightly coupling UI to gameplay classes creates circular dependencies and makes it impossible to change the display without touching gameplay logic.

children.title: Pause and Restart Logic
children.description: Pausing requires setting Time.timeScale to zero and disabling or ignoring input, while restart requires resetting state without reloading the scene when possible. Incomplete pause implementations leave animations, particles, and coroutines running, breaking immersion.

children.title: Resolution Independence
children.description: Canvas Scaler's Scale With Screen Size mode adjusts UI layout to any resolution while preserving designer intent. Without it, a UI designed at 1080p becomes unusably tiny on 4K screens or overflows on mobile devices.

children.title: Buttons, Sliders, and Input Fields
children.description: Unity's built-in Selectable components handle navigation, hover, pressed, and disabled visual states automatically when configured correctly. Building custom click detection with raycasts bypasses all accessibility and controller navigation support.

unity.title: Animation and State Machines
unity.description: This layer covers the Animator controller, blend trees, and event-driven animation. Animation brings characters and feedback to life and signals state to the player.
unity.challenge: Animate the player with idle, run, and jump states driven by movement.

children.title: Animator Controllers and Parameters
children.description: The Animator Controller is a state machine where transitions are triggered by parameters (bool, int, float, trigger). Hardcoding animation state names as strings in scripts causes silent failures when an animator state is renamed, breaking all character animations at runtime.

children.title: Transitions and Conditions
children.description: Transition conditions and the Has Exit Time setting determine when one animation state switches to another. Leaving Has Exit Time on for combat animations forces players to wait through a full clip before an action responds, making combat feel unresponsive.

children.title: Blend Trees for Movement
children.description: A 1D or 2D Blend Tree blends multiple animation clips based on a float parameter, enabling smooth transitions between walk, run, and strafe directions. Without blend trees, characters snap between animations abruptly, which looks amateurish in any movement system.

children.title: Root Motion Basics
children.description: Root motion drives a character's position from the animation clip itself rather than from code, keeping movement and animation perfectly synchronized. Misunderstanding when to enable Apply Root Motion causes characters to slide in place or teleport when animation and code-driven movement conflict.

children.title: Animation Events and Callbacks
children.description: Animation Events call a named method on a MonoBehaviour at a specific frame of a clip, which is the correct way to sync sound effects and hit boxes to animation. Polling animation state in Update to detect when to play sounds creates one-frame delays and frame-rate-dependent timing errors.

children.title: Animation Clips and Keyframes
children.description: Animation clips store time-keyed curves for any serialized property, not just transforms — materials, component values, and activation state are all animatable. Not knowing this leads developers to write scripted sequences in coroutines that are harder to tune and preview than clip-based animation.

children.title: Timeline for Cutscenes
children.description: The Timeline asset sequences animation, audio, camera, and activation tracks for non-interactive cutscenes without writing code. Scripting cutscenes manually produces fragile coroutine chains that break when clip lengths change during production.

children.title: Animation Rigging
children.description: The Animation Rigging package adds procedural constraints — aim, two-bone IK, multi-parent — layered on top of keyframed animation at runtime. Without it, aiming a character's head at a moving target requires either baking many aim variants or writing complex math by hand.

unity.title: Audio and Visual Effects
unity.description: This layer adds sound, music, and particle effects to deepen immersion. Polished feedback through audio and effects makes mechanics feel satisfying.
unity.challenge: Add footstep sounds, background music, and a particle burst on pickups.

children.title: AudioSource and AudioListener
children.description: Every sound in Unity requires an AudioSource component to play and an AudioListener (typically on the camera) to hear it. Having multiple AudioListeners in a scene produces a warning and incorrect stereo panning; missing one means no audio output at all.

children.title: Audio Mixers and Volume Groups
children.description: Audio Mixers route sounds through groups (Music, SFX, Voice) so volume, effects, and snapshots can be controlled independently at runtime. Without mixers, adjusting volume for one category requires iterating over every AudioSource, which is error-prone and breaks whenever new sounds are added.

children.title: Particle System Modules
children.description: The Shuriken Particle System exposes dozens of modules — emission, shape, velocity, color over lifetime — each controlling a different aspect of an effect. Leaving all modules at default produces generic, identical-looking effects that do not communicate gameplay information to the player.

children.title: Effect Pooling for Performance
children.description: Spawning and destroying particle GameObjects every frame generates garbage and frame spikes. Pooling particle systems and using Stop with Clear to reset them is mandatory for mobile and any game with frequent effects.

children.title: 3D Spatial Audio
children.description: Setting an AudioSource's Spatial Blend to 1 enables 3D audio that falls off with distance and pans with direction, essential for immersive environments. Leaving all sounds at 2D means explosions sound equally loud from across the map and there is no audio directional cue for enemies.

children.title: Audio Snapshots
children.description: Mixer snapshots capture a preset of volume and effect settings and can be transitioned between smoothly at runtime, enabling ambient changes like entering a cave or pausing. Scripting these transitions manually with lerped volume values duplicates engine functionality and rarely sounds as smooth.

children.title: VFX Graph
children.description: The Visual Effect Graph runs on the GPU and handles millions of particles that the CPU-based Particle System cannot, making it essential for large-scale effects in URP and HDRP projects. Using the legacy system for hero effects in modern pipelines wastes GPU headroom and limits visual quality.

children.title: Trail and Line Renderers
children.description: Trail Renderers attach to moving objects and leave a fading trail, while Line Renderers draw arbitrary world-space paths — both are essential for projectile trails, ability indicators, and laser effects. Implementing these effects with a chain of spawned quads is orders of magnitude slower.

unity.title: Shader Graph and Materials
unity.description: This layer introduces node-based shaders and the rendering pipeline for custom visuals. Shaders let you craft a unique look without writing low-level code first.
unity.challenge: Create a dissolve shader for enemies and a glowing material for pickups.

children.title: Universal Render Pipeline Setup
children.description: URP requires a Render Pipeline Asset assigned in Project Settings; without it the scene renders magenta and no Shader Graph shaders work. Understanding pipeline assets and forward versus deferred rendering paths is a prerequisite before writing any shader.

children.title: Shader Graph Nodes
children.description: Shader Graph exposes the rendering math as a node network — Sample Texture2D, Lerp, Fresnel Effect — that compiles to HLSL. Not understanding node data types (float, Vector2, Vector4) causes silent type-mismatch errors where a node outputs black or white instead of the expected value.

children.title: Materials and Shader Properties
children.description: A Material is an instance of a Shader with specific property values; exposing properties as Blackboard entries in Shader Graph makes them tunable per-material in the Inspector. Baking property values directly into the graph means creating a new shader for every color or texture variant, inflating build size.

children.title: Texture Sampling and UVs
children.description: UV coordinates map a 2D texture onto a 3D surface, and manipulating them — tiling, scrolling, rotation — is fundamental to most material effects. Misunderstanding UV space causes textures to stretch, repeat incorrectly, or fail to scroll when animating water or lava.

children.title: Dissolve and Fresnel Effects
children.description: A dissolve shader uses a noise texture compared to a threshold value to clip pixels, creating a burning or disintegration effect. The Fresnel node highlights silhouette edges and is the foundation of force field, hologram, and energy shield effects — both are must-know patterns for game artists.

children.title: Vertex Displacement
children.description: Moving vertices in the vertex stage of a shader allows animated geometry — grass swaying, water waves, flag flutter — without any CPU overhead or mesh updates. Animating mesh vertices via script or animation clips instead uses the CPU and cannot scale to dense foliage or large water planes.

children.title: Lighting in URP
children.description: URP uses a forward rendering path with a limited number of real-time lights per object, defined by the pipeline asset settings. Exceeding light limits silently drops lights, and not understanding additional light layers causes confusing visual inconsistencies in complex scenes.

children.title: Post Process Volume
children.description: Post Process Volumes apply screen-space effects — bloom, depth of field, tonemapping — to a camera's output within a defined blend zone. Not using volumes means hardcoding effects per camera, which makes it impossible to transition between interior and exterior looks without code.

unity.title: Data, Saving, and Scriptable Objects
unity.description: This layer covers persisting player progress and architecting data with Scriptable Objects. Clean data design keeps larger projects maintainable and decoupled.
unity.challenge: Add a save and load system that persists score and unlocked levels.

children.title: ScriptableObject Assets
children.description: ScriptableObjects are data containers that live as project assets, shareable across scenes without MonoBehaviour overhead. Using MonoBehaviour components or static classes for shared data instead couples game objects together and makes unit testing nearly impossible.

children.title: Event Channels with ScriptableObjects
children.description: Event channel assets (a ScriptableObject with an action and a raise method) allow completely decoupled communication between gameplay systems. Without them, systems must hold direct references to each other, creating a web of dependencies that breaks when any one system is changed.

children.title: JSON Serialization
children.description: JsonUtility serializes plain C# classes marked with [Serializable] to and from JSON strings for human-readable save files. Using PlayerPrefs to store complex structured data like inventory leads to fragile key-naming schemes that break when new fields are added.

children.title: PlayerPrefs and Save Files
children.description: PlayerPrefs stores simple key-value pairs (string, float, int) suitable for settings but not for structured game data. Misusing PlayerPrefs for complex saves results in data loss on key collisions and makes migrating save formats between game versions extremely difficult.

children.title: Inventory and Item Data Design
children.description: Defining items as ScriptableObject assets allows designers to create new items without touching code, and ensures items share a reference rather than duplicating data across saves. Hard-coding item data as enums or strings makes adding new items a code change, slowing down design iteration.

children.title: Loading and Validating Saves
children.description: Save files must be validated on load to handle missing fields, version mismatches, or corrupted data gracefully. Loading raw JSON without validation causes null reference exceptions that crash the game for players with old save files after an update.

children.title: Service Locator and Dependency Injection
children.description: A service locator or dependency injection container provides systems with their dependencies without hard-coded GetComponent chains or singleton abuse. Without this, large projects accumulate a tangle of singleton references that make testing and refactoring prohibitively expensive.

children.title: Observer Pattern with UnityEvents
children.description: UnityEvents and C# events allow systems to broadcast state changes without knowing who is listening, enabling loose coupling between gameplay and UI. Systems that call methods directly on other systems create fragile one-to-one wiring that breaks whenever a listener is added or removed.

unity.title: AI and Navigation
unity.description: This layer builds enemy behavior using the NavMesh pathfinding and simple state machines. Believable AI raises challenge and makes the world feel reactive.
unity.challenge: Add patrolling enemies that chase the player when spotted using NavMesh.

children.title: NavMesh Baking
children.description: A NavMesh is a pre-computed walkable surface map that the pathfinding agent uses to plan routes. Forgetting to rebake after changing level geometry produces invisible walls or allows agents to walk through obstacles, breaking all AI navigation silently.

children.title: NavMeshAgent Movement
children.description: The NavMeshAgent component handles path planning, steering, and obstacle avoidance automatically when given a destination via SetDestination. Manually moving agents with Rigidbody or Transform while a NavMeshAgent is active causes the agent to fight the physics system, producing jitter.

children.title: Patrol and Chase States
children.description: A patrol state moves an agent between waypoints while a chase state overrides the destination to the player. Without a proper state machine, mixing these behaviors in a single Update method creates spaghetti conditionals that produce erratic AI behavior when edge cases arise.

children.title: Finite State Machines in Unity
children.description: Implementing an FSM as an enum-driven switch or polymorphic state objects keeps AI logic organized and extensible. Writing all AI states as nested if-else chains in a single MonoBehaviour makes it impossible to add a new behavior without risking breaking every existing one.

children.title: Line of Sight Detection
children.description: True line-of-sight requires a raycast from the enemy to the player to verify no obstacles block the path, not just a distance check. Using only distance-based detection makes enemies see through walls, which destroys stealth gameplay and makes the AI feel broken.

resources.label: Sebastian Lague: Field of View Tutorial

children.title: Spawning and Wave Systems
children.description: A wave system controls when and how many enemies appear, scaling difficulty while pooling spawned objects to avoid runtime allocation spikes. Instantiating enemies without pooling generates garbage and frame drops, which is most noticeable on mobile platforms during intense encounters.

children.title: NavMesh Obstacles and Dynamic Updates
children.description: NavMeshObstacle components carve holes into the NavMesh at runtime for moving obstacles, keeping pathfinding accurate without rebaking. Ignoring dynamic obstacles causes agents to path through moving doors or crates, making the game world feel unresponsive.

children.title: NavMesh Links for Multi-Level Navigation
children.description: NavMesh Links connect separate NavMesh surfaces — different floors, jump points, ladders — so agents can path across gaps the automatic bake would not connect. Without links, enemies are forever stranded on their starting floor no matter how close the player is.

unity.title: Optimization and Publishing
unity.description: This layer focuses on profiling, optimizing, and building for target platforms. Shipping a stable performant build is the final step that turns a project into a product.
unity.challenge: Profile and optimize the game, then produce a shippable Windows build.

children.title: Unity Profiler and Frame Debugger
children.description: The Unity Profiler records CPU, GPU, memory, and audio costs per frame, while the Frame Debugger shows every draw call in sequence. Without profiling, developers guess at performance problems and optimize the wrong things, often making the game slower.

children.title: Draw Call Batching
children.description: Static and dynamic batching combine multiple meshes into a single draw call, dramatically reducing CPU overhead when many objects share a material. Ignoring batching on mobile can produce scenes with hundreds of draw calls that exceed GPU budget on low-end devices.

children.title: Object Pooling and Memory
children.description: Reusing GameObjects from a pool instead of calling Instantiate and Destroy eliminates per-frame garbage collection spikes that cause stutter. On mobile platforms, a single unmanaged burst of allocations can trigger a full GC pause long enough for the player to notice.

children.title: Quality and Graphics Tiers
children.description: Unity's Quality Settings let you define presets for shadow resolution, anti-aliasing, and texture quality that can be switched per platform or in settings menus. Without tiers, every player runs the same settings and low-end players get an unplayable experience.

children.title: Build Settings Per Platform
children.description: Each platform target (PC, Android, iOS, WebGL) requires its own Build Settings configuration including texture compression, scripting backend, and architecture. Shipping a build with default settings can produce an uncompressed 2 GB APK or a WebGL build that exhausts mobile RAM.

children.title: Store Submission Basics
children.description: Publishing to Steam, Google Play, or the App Store each requires specific metadata, signing certificates, and content ratings. Missing any step blocks submission, and not reading platform-specific guidelines results in rejections that delay launch by weeks.

children.title: LOD and Culling
children.description: Level of Detail groups swap a mesh to lower-poly versions at distance, and occlusion culling skips rendering objects blocked by other geometry. Not using LOD on complex meshes destroys performance on mid-range hardware because the GPU renders full-detail assets even when they are a few pixels on screen.

children.title: Addressables and Asset Management
children.description: The Addressable Asset System loads and unloads assets by label at runtime, enabling DLC, hot patching, and lean initial downloads. Bundling all assets into a single build produces unnecessarily large downloads and forces users to wait for content they may never access.

unreal.title: Editor and Blueprint Basics
unreal.description: This layer introduces the Unreal editor and visual scripting with Blueprints. These fundamentals let you prototype gameplay before writing any C++.
unreal.challenge: Create a level with a Blueprint actor that rotates and reacts to overlap.

children.title: Viewport Navigation and Panels
children.description: The Unreal editor viewport is your primary window into the world. Without understanding perspective, ortho views, and panel layouts, you will constantly fight the tool instead of building your game.

children.title: Play In Editor Workflow
children.description: PIE lets you test gameplay without leaving the editor, dramatically shortening the iteration loop. Misunderstanding PIE versus Simulate versus Standalone can cause confusing state bugs where values reset unexpectedly.

children.title: Blueprint Event Graphs
children.description: Event graphs execute logic in response to engine events and input. If you skip understanding execution flow and pin types, your Blueprint logic will have silent failures that are nearly impossible to trace.

children.title: Variables, Functions, and Macros
children.description: Variables hold state, functions encapsulate reusable logic, and macros expand inline. Confusing these three causes spaghetti graphs and state management bugs that grow exponentially as projects scale.

children.title: Actors, Components, and the World Outliner
children.description: Actors are the fundamental building blocks of every Unreal scene, and components define what an actor can do. Misunderstanding the actor-component relationship leads to monolithic actors that are impossible to extend or debug.

children.title: Levels, World Outliner, and Persistence
children.description: Levels contain actors and can be streamed in or out at runtime. Ignoring level streaming and persistent versus sub-level concepts produces games that cannot manage memory correctly for open worlds.

children.title: Unreal Online Learning Courses
children.description: Epic's own curated courses cover editor workflows, Blueprint scripting, and engine fundamentals in structured paths. Skipping structured learning leads to knowledge gaps that surface as bugs months into a project.

resources.label: Your First Hour in Unreal Engine 5

children.title: Overlap and Interaction Events
children.description: Overlap events fire when two actors share space and are the foundation of pickups, triggers, and zone detection. Missing the distinction between overlap and hit events leads to interaction logic that misfires or never fires at all.

unreal.title: Player Input and Characters
unreal.description: This layer covers the Character class, possession, and the Enhanced Input system. Responsive character control is the core of any Unreal game.
unreal.challenge: Build a third person character with movement, jumping, and a follow camera.

children.title: Pawns, Characters, and Possession
children.description: The Pawn is any actor a player can control, and the Character subclass adds movement, gravity, and a skeletal mesh out of the box. Skipping this hierarchy results in reimplementing features Unreal provides for free, leading to subtle movement bugs.

children.title: Player Controller and Possession
children.description: The Player Controller is the bridge between the player and their pawn, persisting across respawns and level transitions. Confusing which logic belongs in the Controller versus the Pawn breaks respawning, split-screen, and any AI takeover.

children.title: Input Actions and Mapping Contexts
children.description: Enhanced Input decouples gameplay actions from hardware buttons and allows runtime context switching. Sticking to the legacy input system blocks gamepad rebinding, context-sensitive controls, and localized key mapping.

children.title: Character Movement Component
children.description: The Character Movement Component handles walking, jumping, swimming, and flying with replicated authority. Bypassing it for custom movement code means losing networked movement prediction and all the tunable parameters that designers rely on.

children.title: Spring Arm and Camera Lag
children.description: The Spring Arm component prevents the camera from clipping through geometry and adds configurable lag for a polished feel. Without it, players will clip the camera into walls constantly, breaking immersion and readability.

children.title: Camera Manager and Blending
children.description: The Player Camera Manager controls transitions, shakes, and active camera selection. Ignoring it means manually lerping transforms when changing camera modes, introducing jitter and complexity that the manager handles automatically.

children.title: Third Person Template Walkthrough
children.description: Epic's third person template demonstrates the canonical Unreal character setup with Enhanced Input, camera, and movement correctly wired together. Reading it before building from scratch prevents you from reinventing patterns the engine already encodes.

children.title: Mathew Wadstein WTF Series
children.description: This long-running video series covers nearly every Unreal Blueprint node with plain explanations of parameters and edge cases. Watching relevant episodes before implementing features saves hours of forum searching when nodes behave unexpectedly.

unreal.title: C++ Gameplay Fundamentals
unreal.description: This layer transitions from Blueprints into Unreal C++ and the reflection system. C++ unlocks performance and control needed for serious gameplay code.
unreal.challenge: Rewrite the character base class in C++ and expose tunable values to Blueprints.

children.title: UCLASS, UPROPERTY, and UFUNCTION Macros
children.description: Unreal's macro system exposes C++ types to the editor, Blueprints, serialization, and garbage collection. Forgetting a UPROPERTY on a pointer means the garbage collector will delete the object while your code still holds a reference, causing crashes.

children.title: Garbage Collection and Object Pointers
children.description: Unreal uses a mark-and-sweep garbage collector that tracks all UObjects via UPROPERTY. Storing raw pointers outside of UPROPERTY or TWeakObjectPtr will produce dangling pointer crashes that only appear under specific timing conditions.

children.title: Constructor, BeginPlay, and Tick
children.description: The constructor runs at editor load and CDO creation, while BeginPlay runs at game start, and Tick runs every frame. Calling gameplay logic in the constructor instead of BeginPlay causes editor crashes and logic errors because no world context exists yet.

children.title: Modules and Build Files
children.description: Unreal projects compile as modules defined by Build.cs files that declare public and private dependencies. A missing module dependency produces cryptic linker errors that beginners waste days debugging when the fix is a single line in Build.cs.

children.title: Exposing C++ to Blueprints
children.description: Using BlueprintCallable and BlueprintImplementableEvent allows designers to call C++ logic and override behavior without touching code. Without this bridge, teams either over-engineer everything in C++ or cram too much logic into Blueprints where it cannot be tested.

children.title: Tom Looman Unreal C++ Course
children.description: This publicly available course by Tom Looman (Epic partner developer) is the most practical introduction to Unreal C++ patterns used in production. It demonstrates correct use of the reflection system, timers, and component patterns in real context.

children.title: Live Coding and Hot Reload
children.description: Unreal supports Live Coding to recompile C++ while the editor is running, reducing iteration time dramatically. Using full recompile every change instead of Live Coding costs minutes per iteration and breaks flow on larger codebases.

resources.label: Setting Up Visual Studio for Unreal

children.title: Logging and Output Log
children.description: UE_LOG is the primary debugging tool in Unreal C++ and outputs categorized messages to the Output Log panel. Without structured logging, tracking down bugs in asynchronous or networked code becomes guesswork.

unreal.title: Collisions and Physics
unreal.description: This layer explores collision channels, traces, and the Chaos physics system. Reliable collision and physics drive combat, interaction, and destruction.
unreal.challenge: Add a projectile that traces hits and applies damage to physics actors.

children.title: Collision Channels and Presets
children.description: Collision channels define what objects interact with what, and presets bundle those responses into reusable profiles. Without understanding channels, projectiles will hit invisible walls, UI will block raycasts, and overlap events will fire on the wrong objects.

children.title: Line Traces and Sphere Sweeps
children.description: Trace functions cast rays or shapes through the world and return hit information including the actor, component, normal, and impact point. Using overlapping volumes when you need precise hit detection wastes CPU and produces visually incorrect results.

children.title: Physics Simulation on Actors
children.description: Enabling Simulate Physics on a component hands control of that object to the Chaos solver, which applies gravity, forces, and collision response. Mixing physics simulation with manual transform manipulation creates jitter and undefined behavior.

children.title: Damage and Apply Force
children.description: Unreal provides a damage system with ApplyDamage, ApplyRadialDamage, and the TakeDamage event that decouples the source of damage from the receiving actor. Bypassing this system leads to spaghetti health code that cannot handle shields, invulnerability, or damage types.

children.title: OnComponentBeginOverlap vs OnComponentHit
children.description: Overlap events fire when two shapes intersect without physical resistance, while Hit events fire only when collision is set to block and a physical impact occurs. Mixing these up causes pickups to never trigger or enemies to teleport through walls.

children.title: Physics Constraints
children.description: Physics constraints join two bodies together with configurable angular and linear limits, enabling ragdolls, hinges, and ropes. Without constraints, complex physical assemblies like destructible doors or chains require manual joint logic that diverges from realistic behavior.

children.title: Geometry Collections and Fracture
children.description: Chaos Destruction uses Geometry Collections to pre-fracture meshes into rigid body clusters that break on demand. Attempting to simulate destruction with separate actors instead of Geometry Collections scales poorly and lacks the cluster damage propagation system.

children.title: Physics Materials and Friction
children.description: Physics Materials control friction, restitution, and density per surface, making ice slippery, rubber grippy, and metal bouncy. Without them, all surfaces behave identically and tuning character movement on different terrain types becomes impossible.

unreal.title: UI with UMG
unreal.description: This layer teaches building menus and HUDs with the Unreal Motion Graphics system. Strong UI communicates state and ties mechanics into a full game loop.
unreal.challenge: Add a health bar, main menu, and game over widget to the project.

children.title: Widget Blueprint Anatomy
children.description: Widget Blueprints combine a visual designer canvas with a Graph for logic, enabling complex reactive UI without a separate UI framework. Not understanding the Designer versus Graph split causes developers to put layout code in the graph and logic code in the designer, making both unmaintainable.

children.title: Anchors and Layout Panels
children.description: Anchors define how a widget scales and repositions relative to its parent when the screen resolution changes. Skipping anchors means UI elements that look perfect at 1080p will be off-screen or overlapping at 1440p or ultrawide resolutions.

children.title: Binding Data to Widgets
children.description: Property bindings and event-driven updates decouple game state from its visual representation on screen. Using a Tick-driven polling approach instead of bindings wastes CPU and couples the HUD tightly to game objects, breaking when those objects are destroyed.

children.title: HUD Class and Viewport
children.description: The HUD class provides a low-level canvas drawing API and is the preferred place to manage which widgets are currently visible. Directly spawning widgets from the Player Controller without a HUD manager creates lifecycle issues when switching game states.

children.title: Menu Widgets and Button Events
children.description: Main menus and pause screens are widget blueprints added to the viewport with custom Z-order. Failing to manage input mode (UI only vs Game and UI) when showing menus leaves the player unable to click buttons while the camera still rotates in the background.

children.title: Progress Bars and Health Displays
children.description: The Progress Bar widget maps a 0-1 float to a filled visual and supports animated tween transitions. Building health bars with manually positioned images instead of the Progress Bar misses accessibility features and breaks with non-standard aspect ratios.

children.title: Animations in UMG
children.description: UMG widget animations let you keyframe position, opacity, scale, and color for smooth transitions between UI states. Manually lerping widget properties in the event graph instead of using UMG animations is brittle and requires ticking the widget every frame.

children.title: Common UI for Multi-Platform
children.description: Common UI is Epic's plugin that handles platform-aware button icons, input routing, and menu navigation stacks across console and PC. Shipping without it means manually managing which widget layer is active and which input device the player is using.

unreal.title: Animation and Skeletal Meshes
unreal.description: This layer covers animation blueprints, blend spaces, and state machines for characters. Quality animation sells movement and gives combat its impact.
unreal.challenge: Set up a locomotion state machine and an attack montage for the character.

children.title: Animation Blueprint Event Graph and AnimGraph
children.description: The Animation Blueprint splits logic between an Event Graph for data updates and an AnimGraph for pose evaluation. Doing heavy calculations inside the AnimGraph instead of the Event Graph runs them on the animation thread every frame, causing performance hitches on complex characters.

children.title: State Machines for Locomotion
children.description: Animation state machines define when to transition between clips like idle, walk, run, and jump using conditions on float and bool variables. Without state machines, animation transitions will pop instead of blend, breaking the illusion of continuous motion.

children.title: Blend Spaces for Directional Movement
children.description: Blend Spaces interpolate between multiple animations along one or two axes, typically speed and direction, for smooth locomotion. Hardcoding individual clip transitions instead of blend spaces produces visible pops when diagonal or backward movement is added.

children.title: Montages and Animation Notifies
children.description: Montages play one-shot animations like attacks or rolls on top of the locomotion state machine and support notifies to trigger gameplay events at precise frames. Without montages, playing attack animations requires interrupting the base state machine, breaking blending with movement.

children.title: Skeletal Mesh and Skeleton Assets
children.description: A Skeleton asset defines the bone hierarchy shared by all animations and meshes using that rig. Mixing incompatible skeletons without retargeting causes animations to apply incorrectly, distorting the mesh or snapping bones to wrong positions.

children.title: IK Rig and Animation Retargeting
children.description: IK Retargeting maps animation poses from one skeleton to another, allowing Marketplace or Mixamo animations to work on any character. Without retargeting, teams must recreate every animation for each new character skeleton, a process that scales horribly.

children.title: Control Rig for Procedural Animation
children.description: Control Rig is Unreal's node-based rigging system that enables procedural IK, foot placement, and in-engine character control. Using static animation clips for all situations means characters float above uneven terrain and hands miss grab targets.

children.title: Full Body IK Solver
children.description: The Full Body IK solver adjusts an entire skeleton to satisfy multiple end-effector targets simultaneously, essential for climbing and procedural reactions. Without FBIK, grounding characters on uneven terrain requires hand-authored animations for every slope angle.

unreal.title: Materials and Nanite
unreal.description: This layer dives into the material editor plus Nanite geometry and Lumen lighting. Modern rendering features deliver high fidelity scenes efficiently.
unreal.challenge: Build a Nanite environment with Lumen lighting and a custom material instance.

children.title: Material Editor and Node Graph
children.description: Unreal materials are shader graphs compiled into HLSL that define every visual property of a surface. Not understanding material domains and blend modes means creating materials that render incorrectly in specific lighting conditions or with transparency.

children.title: Material Instances and Parameters
children.description: Material Instances inherit from a parent material and override only exposed parameters, avoiding expensive recompiles for per-object variations. Creating a new full material for each color or roughness variation instead of using instances bloats the shader permutation count and causes cook time explosions.

children.title: PBR Inputs: Base Color, Roughness, Metalness
children.description: Physically Based Rendering standardizes material inputs so surfaces respond correctly to any light source or environment. Treating Unreal materials like hand-painted flat shaders produces surfaces that look accurate under one lighting setup but break under all others.

children.title: Texture Compression and Settings
children.description: Texture compression format and mipmap generation settings have a major impact on GPU memory usage and visual quality at distance. Using uncompressed textures or incorrect sRGB settings causes color banding, memory bloat, and dramatically increased load times.

children.title: Nanite Overview and Limitations
children.description: Nanite streams and renders only the visible micropolygon detail needed for the current view, eliminating manual LOD creation for static meshes. Enabling Nanite on skinned meshes, splines, or masked materials without checking support causes rendering artifacts or silent fallback to lower-quality paths.

children.title: Lumen Global Illumination
children.description: Lumen is a fully dynamic global illumination and reflection system that traces rays in screen space and the scene's SDF. Baking static lightmaps for Lumen-enabled projects wastes build time and conflicts with Lumen's dynamic updates, producing double-lit areas.

children.title: Post Process Volumes
children.description: Post Process Volumes let you blend exposure, color grading, bloom, and depth of field settings per area of the map. Applying global post process settings from a single unbound volume prevents per-zone atmosphere changes and blocks lighting artists from controlling individual areas.

children.title: Temporal Super Resolution
children.description: TSR is Unreal 5's default upscaling technique that renders at a lower resolution and reconstructs fine detail across frames to hit target performance. Disabling TSR to run native resolution for screenshots while leaving it on in performance builds produces inconsistent visual quality comparisons.

unreal.title: Gameplay Ability System
unreal.description: This layer introduces the Gameplay Ability System for abilities, attributes, and effects. GAS provides a scalable framework for complex combat and RPG mechanics.
unreal.challenge: Implement a dash ability and a health attribute using the ability system.

children.title: Ability System Component and Attribute Sets
children.description: The Ability System Component is the hub that owns abilities, attributes, and active effects for an actor. Placing attribute logic outside GAS in separate components prevents the effect system from modifying them, breaking damage, healing, and status effects.

children.title: Gameplay Abilities and Activation
children.description: Gameplay Abilities encapsulate player and AI actions with built-in support for cooldowns, costs, and cancellation. Implementing abilities as Blueprint functions or components instead means reimplementing cooldown timers, input blocking, and animation montage management from scratch.

children.title: Gameplay Effects for Stat Modification
children.description: Gameplay Effects apply instant or duration-based modifications to attributes using magnitude calculation modes including scalable floats and attribute-based scaling. Using SetByCallerMagnitude incorrectly or forgetting to end infinite effects causes attributes to drift permanently during a play session.

children.title: Gameplay Tags for State and Classification
children.description: Gameplay Tags are hierarchical string identifiers used to classify abilities, block activation, and query state without enum coupling. Using booleans or enums for ability state instead of tags breaks tag queries, prevents GAS from blocking conflicting abilities, and requires brittle switch statements.

children.title: Tranek GAS Documentation
children.description: The community-maintained GASDocumentation GitHub repository is the most complete reference for GAS internals, replication, and advanced patterns, far exceeding the official docs in depth. Skipping it leaves developers reinventing solutions to known network replication and prediction problems that are already documented.

children.title: Lyra Starter Game as GAS Reference
children.description: Lyra is Epic's production-quality sample project that uses GAS, Enhanced Input, CommonUI, and modular gameplay plugins together. Studying its architecture before designing your own GAS setup prevents common mistakes around initialization order and input consumption.

children.title: Cooldown and Cost Gameplay Effects
children.description: GAS handles cooldowns and costs as special Gameplay Effects applied to the caster at ability activation, which the UI can query via tag presence. Hard-coding cooldown floats on the ability class instead breaks multiplayer prediction, shared cooldowns, and any effect that should reset or modify cooldown duration.

children.title: Ability Tasks for Async Gameplay
children.description: Ability Tasks are asynchronous latent nodes that wait for events like animation notify or targeting confirmation before continuing ability execution. Polling in Tick or using Delay nodes instead of Ability Tasks bypasses GAS replication and makes ability logic order-dependent on frame timing.

unreal.title: Multiplayer and Replication
unreal.description: This layer covers the Unreal networking model, replication, and RPCs. Networked play is a defining strength of Unreal for multiplayer titles.
unreal.challenge: Make the character and projectile replicate correctly in a listen server session.

children.title: Client-Server Model and Authority
children.description: In Unreal networking the server has authority over the game state, and clients send input requests that the server validates. Modifying authoritative state directly on a client produces desync that manifests as rubberbanding, ghost hits, and players teleporting back to previous positions.

children.title: Replicated Properties
children.description: Marking a property with UPROPERTY(Replicated) tells the server to push its value to all clients whenever it changes. Forgetting to mark properties causes clients to see stale or default values while the server has the correct game state, breaking health bars, score counters, and position.

children.title: Server, Client, and NetMulticast RPCs
children.description: RPCs let clients request server execution and the server broadcast events to all clients, with reliability options for guaranteed delivery. Using a Server RPC without the WithValidation specifier bypasses cheat detection, and using a reliable RPC for frequent events causes network saturation.

children.title: Net Roles and HasAuthority
children.description: Every replicated actor has a network role (Authority, AutonomousProxy, SimulatedProxy) that determines which machine owns and simulates it. Executing server-only logic on all roles causes duplicate events, double damage, and state inconsistencies that are extremely hard to reproduce and fix.

children.title: Character Movement Replication and Prediction
children.description: The Character Movement Component runs client-side prediction and server reconciliation automatically when used correctly. Overriding movement by setting the actor location directly instead of using Move requests destroys prediction, causing jarring corrections every time the server disagrees with the client.

children.title: Listen Servers and Dedicated Servers
children.description: A listen server runs a player and the server on the same machine while a dedicated server runs headless. Assuming listen server behavior in dedicated server builds causes crashes from missing PlayerControllers, HUDs, and local player objects that do not exist on headless servers.

children.title: Networking Compendium Reference
children.description: Cedric Neukirchen's Unreal Networking Compendium is a comprehensive community guide covering replication, RPCs, relevancy, and prediction patterns used in production multiplayer games. Reading it before designing any networked system prevents the most common architectural mistakes that are expensive to undo later.

children.title: Network Relevancy and Prioritization
children.description: Unreal's relevancy system skips replicating actors to clients that are too far away or behind the server, reducing bandwidth. Ignoring relevancy in large open-world games floods clients with updates for hundreds of off-screen actors, causing lag and bandwidth cap violations.

unreal.title: Packaging and Optimization
unreal.description: This layer focuses on profiling with Unreal Insights and packaging shippable builds. Optimizing and packaging is the last mile that delivers a playable product.
unreal.challenge: Profile the game with Insights and produce an optimized packaged build.

children.title: Unreal Insights Trace System
children.description: Unreal Insights records CPU, GPU, memory, and network events into a trace file that can be opened in the Timing Insights viewer. Using console stat commands alone gives averages but misses single-frame spikes and async task contention that Insights captures precisely.

children.title: GPU Profiling and RenderDoc
children.description: The GPU Visualizer (r.ProfileGPU) and RenderDoc integration identify which render passes consume the most GPU time per frame. Optimizing CPU code when the bottleneck is a GPU overdraw or shadow cascade rebuild wastes time and does not improve frame rate.

children.title: Level of Detail and HLOD
children.description: Static mesh LODs reduce triangle count for distant objects, and Hierarchical LODs merge entire clusters of meshes into a single draw call at far ranges. Shipping without LODs causes GPU vertex processing to spike when the camera pulls back to show large areas of the map.

resources.label: Level of Detail for Static Meshes

children.title: Occlusion Culling and Visibility
children.description: Unreal's occlusion system skips rendering actors whose bounds are fully occluded by other geometry, reducing draw calls. Disabling or incorrectly sizing bounds on actors defeats the culling system and forces the GPU to render thousands of objects that will never be seen.

children.title: Project Packaging and Cooking
children.description: Cooking converts Unreal assets into platform-specific binary formats and packaging bundles them with the engine for distribution. Shipping the Development configuration instead of Shipping leaves console commands, profiling overhead, and debug symbols in the final build, increasing size and slowing performance.

children.title: Platform Target Settings
children.description: Each target platform has settings for rendering quality tiers, texture compression formats, and feature level support that must be configured before packaging. Ignoring platform settings means the package will use the editor's development defaults, producing oversized builds that may not run on the target hardware.

children.title: Asset Auditor and Memory Budget
children.description: The Asset Audit window shows texture memory, mesh triangle counts, and asset sizes across the entire project in one view. Flying blind on memory usage causes runtime out-of-memory crashes on target platforms that have far less RAM than a development workstation.

children.title: Scalability Settings and Quality Tiers
children.description: Unreal's scalability system maps engine.ini cvar groups to Low, Medium, High, and Epic quality buckets that players or the game can select at runtime. Hardcoding quality-related cvars instead of using the scalability system prevents players from adjusting settings and blocks console certification that requires graphics presets.

web-game.title: JavaScript and Canvas Basics
web-game.description: This layer establishes JavaScript fundamentals and drawing to the HTML5 canvas. These basics underpin every browser-based game you will build.
web-game.challenge: Draw a player sprite on canvas that moves with arrow keys in a game loop.

children.title: JavaScript Execution Model
children.description: JavaScript is single-threaded and event-driven, so blocking the main thread freezes both rendering and input. Understanding the call stack, task queue, and microtasks lets you write game loops that stay smooth instead of stuttering under load.

children.title: HTML5 Canvas 2D Context
children.description: The CanvasRenderingContext2D API is the foundation of 2D browser games. Misunderstanding the retained-mode versus immediate-mode distinction causes artefacts and wasted draw calls every frame.

children.title: requestAnimationFrame & Delta Time
children.description: requestAnimationFrame syncs updates to the display refresh rate and pauses when the tab is hidden, avoiding runaway loops. Without delta-time scaling, game speed becomes tied to frame rate and breaks on high-refresh monitors.

resources.label: MDN Game Development: anatomy of a video game

children.title: Input Event Handling
children.description: Keyboard and pointer events fire asynchronously outside the render loop. Polling a state map each frame instead of acting in the handler prevents missed or doubled inputs and keeps logic deterministic.

children.title: Static Typing in Game Code
children.description: TypeScript catches interface mismatches between game entities at compile time rather than at runtime. Skipping types in larger projects causes subtle bugs where entity data shapes drift and only fail during specific game states.

children.title: ES Modules & Project Structure
children.description: Splitting game code into ES modules keeps systems decoupled and enables tree-shaking in production bundles. A monolithic script file makes it hard to test systems in isolation and balloons bundle sizes.

children.title: Canvas Coordinate Space
children.description: Canvas uses a top-left origin with y increasing downward, which is the inverse of most math conventions. Confusing screen space with world space leads to flipped physics, wrong collision bounds, and difficult camera math.

children.title: Sprite & Image Rendering
children.description: drawImage with sprite sheets and sub-rectangles avoids creating hundreds of DOM Image elements. Loading images before the first frame and handling the onload event prevents flickering black sprites on game start.

web-game.title: Phaser Game Framework
web-game.description: This layer introduces Phaser for scenes, sprites, and arcade physics. A framework speeds up development and handles common 2D game plumbing.
web-game.challenge: Build a 2D platformer prototype with a tilemap level and arcade physics.

children.title: Scene Lifecycle Methods
children.description: Phaser scenes divide game flow into preload, create, and update phases that map to asset loading, object setup, and per-frame logic. Putting asset loads outside preload or heavy object creation inside update causes loading failures and frame drops.

children.title: Scene Manager & Data Passing
children.description: The SceneManager lets you start, pause, stop, and overlay scenes without destroying the entire game. Ignoring scene data passing forces global state, which makes menus and transitions brittle and hard to test.

children.title: Phaser Sprite & Image Objects
children.description: Sprites add physics bodies and animation support on top of a base image. Using plain images for objects that need physics bodies causes silent failures where collisions never register.

children.title: Static & Dynamic Groups
children.description: Groups with pre-allocated members avoid per-frame garbage collection by reusing pooled objects. Creating new sprites every time an enemy or bullet spawns spikes memory and causes GC pauses that stutter frame pacing.

children.title: Tiled Map Editor & JSON Import
children.description: Tiled exports JSON that Phaser's Tilemap system ingests directly, separating level data from code. Hardcoding platform positions in code makes levels impossible to iterate on quickly and prevents non-programmers from contributing levels.

children.title: Tilemap Collision Layers
children.description: Setting collision on specific tile indices or a separate collision layer keeps physics tight without invisible walls. Colliding with every tile including decorative ones causes the player to catch on visual elements that should be passable.

children.title: Arcade Physics Bodies
children.description: Arcade physics uses AABB and circle colliders optimized for speed rather than realism. Expecting slope or polygon collision from Arcade physics causes clipping bugs; those cases require Matter.js instead.

children.title: Colliders & Overlaps
children.description: collider stops and separates objects while overlap fires a callback without stopping movement. Mixing them up causes enemies to phase through walls or the player to be pushed by pickups that should only trigger a score increase.

web-game.title: Sprites, Animation, and Audio
web-game.description: This layer covers sprite sheets, animations, and sound for richer 2D games. Animation and audio give a web game its feel and feedback.
web-game.challenge: Add animated walk and jump cycles plus sound effects to the platformer.

children.title: Animation Manager & Keys
children.description: The global AnimationManager stores animations by key so any sprite can play them without duplicating frame data. Defining the same animation inside each sprite class wastes memory and makes frame rate changes require edits in multiple places.

children.title: Sprite Sheets & Texture Atlases
children.description: Texture atlases pack multiple sprites into one image, reducing GPU draw calls and HTTP requests. Loading dozens of individual PNG files multiplies round-trips and fragments the texture cache, noticeably slowing initial load.

children.title: AudioContext & Nodes
children.description: The Web Audio API routes audio through a graph of nodes ending at the destination, enabling mixing, filtering, and spatial sound in code. Using HTML audio elements for game sounds limits simultaneous playback and prevents low-latency triggering.

children.title: Autoplay Policy & User Gesture
children.description: Browsers block AudioContext.resume until the user has interacted with the page. Games that start audio immediately fail silently on first load; resuming the context on the first click or keypress is the reliable cross-browser pattern.

children.title: Phaser Particle Emitter
children.description: Phaser's built-in particle emitter handles pooling, lifespan, and property curves without manual object management. Writing a custom particle loop in update re-introduces the garbage collection pauses the emitter is designed to avoid.

children.title: Tweens for Polish
children.description: The TweenManager interpolates any numeric property over time with easing, removing hand-written lerp loops from update. Animating positions manually in update makes it hard to chain sequences or reverse animations on demand.

children.title: Phaser Sound Objects
children.description: Phaser wraps Web Audio or HTML Audio depending on platform, giving a consistent API for play, pause, loop, and volume. Bypassing the Phaser sound manager and touching AudioContext directly breaks the cross-platform abstraction and scene-level muting.

children.title: Audio Sprite & Pooling
children.description: Audio sprites pack many short clips into one file and seek to play each sound, cutting HTTP requests to one. Loading a separate audio file for every effect multiplies network latency and browser decode overhead on startup.

web-game.title: Game State and UI
web-game.description: This layer manages score, lives, and menus across multiple scenes. Clear UI and state flow turn a prototype into a complete game loop.
web-game.challenge: Add a menu, score HUD, and high score saving to the platformer.

children.title: Multi-Scene Architecture
children.description: Keeping UI, gameplay, and menus in separate scenes lets them be layered, restarted, or swapped independently. Putting all game logic in one scene makes it impossible to show a pause overlay without halting the underlying game simulation.

children.title: Game State Machine
children.description: A lightweight state machine separates playing, paused, and game-over states so transitions are explicit and guarded. Ad-hoc boolean flags for each state combination grow exponentially and produce impossible mixed states.

children.title: LocalStorage for High Scores
children.description: localStorage persists key-value strings across sessions without a server. Storing complex objects requires JSON.stringify; skipping that stores [object Object] silently and corrupts saved data on the next read.

children.title: Scene Registry & Event Emitter
children.description: Phaser's scene registry and EventEmitter allow decoupled communication between scenes without shared mutable globals. Passing data through window globals leaks state and causes scores or lives to persist incorrectly after a restart.

children.title: Bitmap Fonts vs. Web Fonts
children.description: Bitmap fonts pre-render glyphs into a texture atlas giving consistent pixel-perfect rendering at any scale. Using Canvas text with web fonts causes layout shifts while fonts load and inconsistent kerning across browsers.

children.title: Scale Manager & Responsive Canvas
children.description: Phaser's ScaleManager handles canvas resizing, device pixel ratio, and fit modes so the game looks correct on all screen sizes. Hardcoding canvas dimensions breaks layout on mobile and causes blurry rendering on high-DPI displays.

children.title: Phaser Input Manager
children.description: Phaser unifies keyboard, mouse, pointer, and gamepad input under one InputManager, handling pointer events correctly across desktop and touch. Attaching raw DOM event listeners inside game code causes duplicate events and skips Phaser's camera-aware coordinate transformation.

children.title: Gamepad Support
children.description: The Gamepad API lets web games work with Xbox, PlayStation, and generic controllers. Ignoring it excludes a large share of casual players and prevents the game from running comfortably on TV-connected browsers.

web-game.title: WebGL and 3D with Three.js
web-game.description: This layer introduces 3D rendering in the browser using Three.js over WebGL. Moving to 3D opens a new dimension of web game possibilities.
web-game.challenge: Build a simple 3D scene with a controllable model and a follow camera.

children.title: Scene, Camera & Renderer
children.description: Every Three.js game starts with a Scene that holds objects, a Camera that defines the view frustum, and a WebGLRenderer that draws to the canvas. Skipping renderer pixel ratio configuration produces blurry output on retina displays and confuses raycaster coordinates.

children.title: Mesh, Geometry & Material
children.description: A Mesh combines a BufferGeometry (vertex data) with a Material (shader + uniforms). Modifying geometry vertices every frame instead of using morph targets or shader uniforms bottlenecks the CPU-GPU data transfer.

children.title: Perspective Camera & FOV
children.description: Field of view and near/far clip planes control what the camera renders and its depth precision. A far plane set too large causes z-fighting artefacts; too small clips distant objects that should be visible.

children.title: 3D Model Loading with GLTFLoader
children.description: GLTF is the web standard for 3D models with animations, materials, and hierarchy in one file. Loading OBJ loses animation data and material PBR properties, requiring manual reconstruction work that GLTF provides for free.

children.title: Light Types & Shadows
children.description: DirectionalLight, PointLight, and SpotLight each have different shadow map setups and performance costs. Enabling shadows on every mesh with default settings tanks frame rate; only meshes that visibly cast shadows should have castShadow enabled.

children.title: Environment Maps & PBR
children.description: MeshStandardMaterial uses a physically based shading model that relies on an environment map for reflections and ambient light. Without an environment map, metallic materials render flat black and the scene loses depth cues.

children.title: AnimationMixer & Clips
children.description: AnimationMixer drives skeletal and morph-target animations stored in GLTF files, advancing them each frame with delta time. Forgetting to call mixer.update(delta) every frame freezes animations at frame zero regardless of the action being set to play.

children.title: Object3D Hierarchy & Transform
children.description: Grouping meshes under parent Object3D nodes lets you transform an entire character or vehicle with one matrix operation. Parenting objects incorrectly causes child positions to compound transforms in unexpected ways that are hard to debug visually.

web-game.title: 3D Physics and Interaction
web-game.description: This layer adds a physics engine and raycasting for 3D interaction. Physics and picking make 3D web games tangible and playable.
web-game.challenge: Add gravity, collisions, and clickable objects to the 3D scene.

children.title: Cannon-es Bodies & World
children.description: cannon-es maintains a separate physics world with bodies that mirror Three.js meshes; you copy the body transform to the mesh each frame. Mismatching the physics body size with the visual mesh causes objects to appear to float, sink, or collide invisibly.

children.title: Fixed Physics Timestep
children.description: Running the physics world with a fixed step decoupled from render frame rate keeps simulation deterministic. Stepping physics with the raw delta time makes high-frame-rate machines run physics faster, breaking collision tunnelling thresholds.

children.title: Three.js Raycaster
children.description: Raycaster projects a ray from camera through a mouse or touch coordinate and returns intersected objects, enabling click-to-select and hit detection. Using the wrong coordinate normalization (-1 to 1 NDC) produces rays that miss all objects silently.

children.title: Collision Events & Contact Materials
children.description: cannon-es fires collide events on bodies and lets you assign contact materials per pair to control friction and restitution. Ignoring contact materials makes all surfaces feel identically slippery or bouncy, undermining game feel tuning.

children.title: Physics-Based Character Movement
children.description: Applying forces or setting velocities directly on a physics body produces character movement that obeys gravity and collision. Moving a character by setting its position directly bypasses the physics world, causing tunnelling through walls at high speed.

children.title: Ground Detection & Jump
children.description: Detecting ground contact correctly requires checking the collision normal angle rather than body velocity, because velocity-based checks fail on ramps and moving platforms. A flawed ground check lets the player double-jump mid-air, breaking intended platforming feel.

children.title: cannon-es Debug Renderer
children.description: Rendering physics body wireframes alongside Three.js meshes confirms that visual and physics shapes are aligned. Shipping without ever enabling debug rendering often hides scale mismatches that cause gameplay to feel inexplicably unfair.

children.title: Stats.js Performance Monitor
children.description: Stats.js overlays FPS, render time, and memory stats onto the canvas during development. Optimizing without measuring produces guesswork fixes that may slow unrelated code paths while the real bottleneck remains untouched.

web-game.title: Shaders and Visual Effects
web-game.description: This layer introduces GLSL shaders and post processing for custom visuals. Shaders give web games a distinct and polished look.
web-game.challenge: Write a custom shader material and add a bloom post processing effect.

children.title: Vertex & Fragment Shader Stages
children.description: The vertex shader positions geometry and passes varyings to the fragment shader, which outputs the final pixel color. Trying to sample a texture in the vertex stage on older WebGL1 hardware silently falls back to black because texture2D in vertex shaders is not guaranteed.

children.title: Uniforms, Attributes & Varyings
children.description: Uniforms are per-draw constants (time, resolution), attributes are per-vertex (position, UV), and varyings interpolate between them for the fragment stage. Confusing their roles causes constants to be rebuilt every vertex or per-pixel data to be sampled only once.

children.title: ShaderMaterial vs RawShaderMaterial
children.description: ShaderMaterial prepends Three.js built-in uniforms and GLSL chunks, while RawShaderMaterial gives full control over the GLSL source. Using RawShaderMaterial without providing precision qualifiers crashes on mobile WebGL contexts that require them.

children.title: Updating Uniforms Each Frame
children.description: Shader uniforms like elapsed time must be updated in the render loop by writing to material.uniforms.uTime.value. Forgetting to update freezes time-based animations at zero even though the shader code is correct.

children.title: EffectComposer & Passes
children.description: Three.js post-processing renders the scene to a render target then applies fullscreen passes for bloom, depth of field, and color grading. Rendering post effects without a render target reads the visible canvas, producing feedback loops and sampling artefacts.

children.title: Bloom & Tone Mapping
children.description: Bloom requires HDR rendering with tone mapping so emissive materials glow correctly without oversaturating the rest of the scene. Applying bloom to an LDR buffer clamps bright values before the blur, producing halos rather than true bloom.

children.title: WebGL Precision & Mobile
children.description: highp precision is not guaranteed in fragment shaders on mobile GPUs; using mediump can produce visible banding. Writing precision-agnostic shaders and testing on a real mobile device catches these degraded-precision artefacts before release.

children.title: Shader Variants & Defines
children.description: GLSL #define constants and Three.js shader defines let one shader cover multiple visual modes without runtime branching cost. Dynamic if statements on non-uniform values force both branches to execute on GPU hardware, wasting cycles.

web-game.title: Networking and Multiplayer
web-game.description: This layer connects browsers to a server for real-time multiplayer. Networking enables shared sessions and competitive web games.
web-game.challenge: Add real-time multiplayer movement to a game using WebSockets.

children.title: WebSocket Connection Lifecycle
children.description: WebSocket establishes a persistent full-duplex channel after an HTTP upgrade handshake, enabling low-latency bidirectional messages. Not handling the onclose and onerror events leaves players stuck on a disconnected session with no way to reconnect.

children.title: Socket.io Rooms & Namespaces
children.description: Socket.io rooms let you scope broadcasts to a lobby or match session without filtering messages client-side. Broadcasting globally instead of to a room leaks game state between separate matches and scales poorly as player count grows.

children.title: Server Authority & Validation
children.description: The server owns the canonical game state and validates all player input before applying it. Trusting client-sent positions directly allows trivial cheating where a modified client teleports or grants invincibility.

children.title: Message Serialization
children.description: Choosing between JSON and binary formats like MessagePack or FlatBuffers trades readability for payload size. Sending full game state as verbose JSON every tick saturates mobile connections and causes visible lag on 3G.

children.title: Client-Side Prediction
