children.description: Client-side prediction applies input locally before server confirmation so movement feels instant regardless of round-trip time. Without it even 50ms latency makes controls feel sluggish and unresponsive because every action waits for a server echo.

children.title: Entity Interpolation
children.description: Interpolating remote entity positions between received snapshots smooths out packet jitter into visually continuous movement. Snapping remote players to each received position produces jittery teleportation that looks like lag even at low ping.

children.title: Room Lifecycle on the Server
children.description: Creating and cleaning up rooms on player connect and disconnect prevents memory leaks from abandoned sessions. Leaking room objects accumulates state in the server process until it crashes under load in production.

children.title: Matchmaking & Player Lists
children.description: Maintaining a player roster per room and broadcasting join/leave events keeps all clients synchronized about who is present. Clients that track their own player list diverge from the server list, causing ghost players that cannot be removed.

web-game.title: Asset Pipeline and Tooling
web-game.description: This layer covers bundling, asset loading, and build tooling for web games. A solid pipeline keeps load times low and the project maintainable.
web-game.challenge: Set up a Vite build with optimized asset loading for the game project.

children.title: Vite for Web Games
children.description: Vite serves ES modules natively in development for instant hot reload and bundles with Rollup for production, eliminating the slow rebuild cycle of older bundlers. Configuring incorrect base paths breaks asset URLs when deploying to a subdirectory on itch.io or a CDN.

children.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
children.description: Dynamic import() splits the bundle so the game does not parse every level's code on startup. Loading the entire game bundle upfront causes a white screen on mobile connections for several seconds before gameplay starts.

children.title: Basis Universal & KTX2 Textures
children.description: Basis Universal transcodes to the GPU's native compressed format at load time, halving texture memory and upload time. Shipping uncompressed PNG textures saturates GPU memory on mobile devices and causes the browser tab to be killed by the OS.

children.title: GLTF Draco Mesh Compression
children.description: Draco compresses GLTF geometry to a fraction of its raw size, dramatically reducing download time for complex models. Sending uncompressed 3D models over the network stalls the loading screen and prevents low-bandwidth players from reaching the game.

children.title: Loading Manager & Progress
children.description: Three.js LoadingManager tracks pending loads and fires onProgress and onLoad callbacks to drive a loading screen. Without it the game renders before models and textures finish loading, showing invisible or placeholder geometry.

children.title: Sprite Atlas Generation
children.description: Generating sprite atlases with TexturePacker or free-tex-packer before build time bakes the optimal packing, reducing draw calls in 2D games. Running atlas generation at runtime in the browser wastes CPU on every page load and adds startup latency.

children.title: Git LFS for Binary Assets
children.description: Git Large File Storage stores textures, audio, and models outside the main repository history, keeping clone times fast. Committing raw binary assets to a standard git repository bloats the pack file until clones and CI times become impractical.

children.title: Asset Pipeline Automation
children.description: Running compression, atlas packing, and format conversion in npm scripts or CI ensures the published build always uses optimized assets. Manual optimization steps that depend on a developer running a tool before each release get skipped under deadline pressure.

web-game.title: Optimization and Deployment
web-game.description: This layer focuses on performance profiling and deploying a web game to the public. Shipping a fast loading playable build completes the journey.
web-game.challenge: Profile, optimize, and deploy the finished game to itch.io or a host.

children.title: Chrome DevTools Performance Panel
children.description: The Performance panel records a timeline of JavaScript execution, layout, paint, and GPU work, pinpointing which function takes the most frame time. Optimizing without profiling leads to micro-optimizing code that accounts for less than 1% of frame time while real bottlenecks remain.

children.title: Frame Budget & Draw Calls
children.description: A 60fps frame budget is 16.6ms; exceeding it causes dropped frames visible as stutter. Reducing draw calls with instancing and batching is often the highest-impact optimization because each call carries significant CPU-to-GPU overhead regardless of geometry complexity.

children.title: Three.js Geometry & Texture Disposal
children.description: Calling geometry.dispose() and texture.dispose() releases GPU memory when objects are removed from the scene. Forgetting to dispose leaks VRAM silently until the browser tab crashes or the GPU runs out of memory during level transitions.

children.title: Object Pooling to Avoid GC
children.description: Reusing pre-allocated bullet, particle, and enemy objects from a pool eliminates per-spawn allocation and reduces garbage collection pauses. Allocating new objects for every bullet in a shoot-em-up triggers GC every few seconds, causing frame spikes that feel like lag.

children.title: Deploying to itch.io
children.description: itch.io hosts HTML5 games in an iframe sandbox, requiring correct MIME types and a single zip with index.html at the root. Uploading a folder structure without an index.html at the root level produces a blank page that itch.io cannot auto-detect.

children.title: CDN & Cache Headers
children.description: Serving game assets from a CDN with long-lived cache headers cuts load time for returning players dramatically. Serving multi-megabyte texture atlases with no-cache headers forces every session to re-download the full bundle regardless of browser cache.

children.title: Progressive Asset Loading
children.description: Loading only the assets required for the current level and streaming the rest keeps initial load under 5 seconds on mobile. Requiring the player to wait while all levels load upfront causes 30-70% of players to abandon before seeing the title screen.

children.title: Service Worker & Offline Play
children.description: A service worker can cache game assets and serve them offline, turning a web game into an installable PWA. Without offline support, any network interruption mid-session crashes the game and loses progress.

game-designer.title: Design Fundamentals
game-designer.description: This layer introduces core design vocabulary, player motivation, and the role of a designer. A shared foundation in theory frames every decision that follows.
game-designer.challenge: Write a one page concept defining the core loop and pillars of a small game.

children.title: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Motivation
children.description: Players driven by intrinsic rewards — mastery, curiosity, autonomy — stay engaged far longer than those chasing external points alone. Misunderstanding this split leads to reward systems that feel hollow and cause players to quit once the novelty wears off.

children.title: Player Types (Bartle Taxonomy)
children.description: Bartle's four player types — Killers, Achievers, Socializers, Explorers — map different motivational profiles that a game's systems must satisfy. Designing for only one type alienates large portions of your audience and creates imbalanced engagement.

children.title: The Core Loop and Pillars
children.description: A core loop is the repeating cycle of actions that defines the second-to-second feel of your game. Without a tight and rewarding loop, no amount of content or polish can rescue a game from feeling aimless.

children.title: MDA Framework
children.description: The Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics framework separates what a designer controls (mechanics) from what emerges in play (dynamics) and what the player feels (aesthetics). Forgetting this chain causes designers to tweak mechanics while being blind to the aesthetic outcome.

children.title: Game Feel and Juice
children.description: Game feel is the tactile quality of player input — the weight, responsiveness, and feedback that makes actions satisfying. A mechanic that works on paper can feel terrible without proper juice, killing player engagement before they understand the design.

resources.label: Juice It or Lose It (GDC Talk)

children.title: Design Pillars
children.description: Design pillars are 3-5 guiding principles that every decision in a game is measured against. Without them, teams drift toward feature creep and inconsistent experiences that confuse players about what kind of game they are playing.

children.title: One Page Design Document
children.description: A one-page document forces radical clarity by constraining the pitch to only what is essential: the hook, the loop, the target audience, and the tone. Designers who cannot write a one-pager typically do not understand their own game well enough to build it.

children.title: Reference and Mood Boards
children.description: Mood boards align team aesthetic vision before a pixel is drawn or a line of code is written. Skipping this step leads to art, audio, and design pulling in different tonal directions, producing a game that feels incoherent.

resources.label: Using Mood Boards in Game Design

game-designer.title: The Game Design Document
game-designer.description: This layer teaches structuring a clear and living game design document. A good GDD aligns the team and turns ideas into actionable plans.
topics: One page and full design docs
game-designer.challenge: Expand the concept into a structured GDD with scope and feature list.

children.title: Sections of a Full GDD
children.description: A full GDD covers overview, mechanics, systems, level design, UI, audio direction, and production scope in organized sections. Without this structure, information lives in designers' heads, causing miscommunication and costly implementation errors.

children.title: Living Documentation
children.description: A GDD is a living artifact that evolves with the game, not a contract set at kickoff. Teams that treat the GDD as unchangeable either abandon it after the first iteration or ship a game that diverges from the actual experience.

children.title: Feature Lists and Scope
children.description: Every feature added to a scope document represents time, budget, and risk. Designers who do not ruthlessly prioritize core features over nice-to-haves typically ship incomplete games or kill projects in development.

children.title: Communicating Design Intent
children.description: A GDD must communicate the why behind every design decision so engineers and artists can make correct judgment calls when details are ambiguous. Documents that list only what to build without why produce technically correct but experientially wrong implementations.

resources.label: Writing Design Docs Devs Will Read

children.title: Notion for Game Design
children.description: Notion provides linked databases, embedded media, and collaborative editing that traditional docs cannot match for tracking a game's evolving design. Using flat text documents for a complex GDD leads to outdated sections, broken cross-references, and friction for the team.

children.title: Figma for Wireframes and Boards
children.description: Figma lets designers create interface wireframes, flow diagrams, and mood boards in a single shared space accessible to the whole team. Designing UI on paper alone creates a gap between intent and implementation that costs significant iteration time.

children.title: Reading Postmortems
children.description: Published postmortems are a dense source of real lessons from shipped games about what planning worked and what failed. Designers who skip postmortem reading are doomed to repeat documented mistakes from the industry's collective experience.

children.title: Writing Your Own Postmortem
children.description: Writing a postmortem after each project forces honest reflection on what worked, what did not, and what you will change — the core habit of a professional designer. Without this habit, designers improve slowly and repeat the same process errors across projects.

game-designer.title: Systems and Mechanics
game-designer.description: This layer explores designing interlocking systems and the rules that govern them. Strong systems thinking creates depth and emergent play.
game-designer.challenge: Design a core mechanic and a supporting progression system for your game.

children.title: Positive and Negative Feedback Loops
children.description: Positive feedback accelerates advantage while negative feedback corrects imbalance; every game system contains both, whether intentional or not. Designers who do not map their loops explicitly ship games with runaway snowballing or frustrating rubber-banding that players experience as unfair.

children.title: Emergent vs Scripted Play
children.description: Emergent play arises from system interactions the designer did not explicitly author; scripted play follows a predetermined path. Over-scripting eliminates player agency while pure emergence can produce chaotic unreadable experiences — the balance defines your design signature.

children.title: Resources, Sources, and Sinks
children.description: Every in-game economy needs sources that produce resources and sinks that consume them; an economy without sinks collapses into infinite surplus. Mismanaging this balance creates hyperinflation or scarcity that makes the game feel broken regardless of how well other systems are designed.

children.title: Progression Systems
children.description: Progression systems give players a sense of growing power and mastery over time, providing a long-term motivational arc beyond the core loop. Poorly paced progression that rewards too fast or too slow kills retention — players either feel done too early or grind without visible growth.

children.title: Rule Clarity and Affordances
children.description: Rules must be learnable from play without requiring external documentation; unclear rules force players to guess or quit. When a player breaks a rule accidentally it almost always means the rule was not properly afforded by the game world or interface.

resources.label: The Basics of Game Design Rules

children.title: Risk and Reward
children.description: Meaningful decisions require a genuine trade-off between potential gain and potential loss; decisions without real risk become mindless repetition. Designers who stack rewards without risk training players to optimize mechanically destroy the tension that makes choices feel significant.

children.title: Machinations for System Modeling
children.description: Machinations lets designers simulate resource flows and feedback loops before implementing them in an engine, catching balance catastrophes at the design stage. Skipping simulation means discovering broken economies only after weeks of engineering work.

children.title: Spreadsheet Modeling
children.description: Spreadsheets let designers model stat curves, damage formulas, and cost tables to find edge cases and broken outliers before they reach players. A designer who cannot model their own numbers in a spreadsheet is flying blind through every balancing pass.

game-designer.title: Level and Encounter Design
game-designer.description: This layer covers pacing, layout, and guiding players through space. Level design shapes the moment to moment experience of a game.
game-designer.challenge: Block out a tutorial level that teaches your core mechanic without text.

children.title: Pacing and Difficulty Curves
children.description: Level pacing controls the rhythm of tension and release that keeps players engaged without burning them out or boring them. Flat difficulty curves — constant tension or constant ease — cause emotional fatigue and players stop playing not because they failed but because they felt nothing.

resources.label: Pacing and Tension in Level Design

children.title: Spatial Layout and Flow
children.description: Spatial layout determines how players navigate and what they perceive at any moment, shaping their attention and decision-making. Poor spatial design creates confusion, backtracking, and frustration that players blame on the whole game rather than one level.

children.title: Tutorials Without Text
children.description: The strongest tutorials teach mechanics through environmental cues, safe consequence spaces, and guided first encounters rather than interrupting play with text pop-ups. Text-heavy tutorials that front-load information are skipped by players who then blame the game for being confusing.

children.title: Critical Path and Secrets
children.description: The critical path is the mandatory route through a level; secrets branch off it and reward curiosity without blocking progress. Designers who bury required content in exploration create frustrated players, while those with no secrets miss rewarding an entire play style.

resources.label: Secrets and Exploration in Level Design

children.title: Encounter Composition
children.description: An encounter is a discrete challenge unit that tests the skills the designer has been teaching; its composition controls difficulty and pacing at a micro level. Encounters that introduce multiple new threats simultaneously overwhelm players and produce arbitrary deaths rather than skill-based failure.

children.title: Guiding the Player
children.description: Environmental guiding uses light, color, architecture, and sound to direct attention without breaking immersion or adding UI arrows. When guiding fails players feel lost, blame the design for being unclear, and rate the game negatively regardless of the quality of everything else.

children.title: Blockouts and Gray Boxing
children.description: Gray boxing a level with untextured geometry reveals flow, scale, and pacing problems before any art is committed, making iteration cheap. Art-first level design locks teams into layouts that are beautiful but unplayable, requiring expensive teardowns late in production.

children.title: Tiled for 2D Layout
children.description: Tiled is the industry-standard 2D tile map editor that exports formats usable in most game engines and frameworks. Designing 2D levels directly in-engine without a dedicated tool leads to slow iteration cycles and no visual overview of the full level structure.

game-designer.title: Prototyping in Unity
game-designer.description: This layer gets designers building playable prototypes in Unity. Prototyping turns paper ideas into testable interactive experiences.
game-designer.challenge: Build a gray box playable prototype of your core mechanic in Unity.

children.title: Gray Box Prototyping in Unity
children.description: A gray box prototype uses primitive geometry, no art, and minimum code to make a single mechanic testable; it answers whether an idea is fun before any production investment. Designers who skip to full production before validating core fun waste months on mechanics that fail their first playtest.

children.title: Iteration Cycles
children.description: Short iteration cycles — build a little, test, change — compound learning exponentially compared to long waterfall development. Designers who prototype for weeks without testing are accumulating risk rather than reducing it.

children.title: Tabletop Prototypes for Digital Games
children.description: Paper prototypes strip a mechanic down to its logical skeleton, revealing rule clarity and balance problems without any code. Designers who start digital before testing on paper often build mechanically sound but experientially broken systems that require deep refactors.

children.title: Placeholder Art Discipline
children.description: Committing to placeholder art discipline — using programmer art throughout prototyping — prevents scope creep and keeps focus on feel rather than aesthetics. Teams that polish art during prototyping lose time on features that may be cut and develop emotional attachment to systems that do not serve the game.

children.title: Unity Editor Basics for Non-Coders
children.description: Designers who understand the Unity editor — GameObjects, components, prefabs, and the inspector — can test and tune their own mechanics without waiting for an engineer. Designers who cannot navigate the engine become bottlenecks and lose the rapid iteration that makes prototyping valuable.

children.title: ProBuilder for Level Blocking
children.description: ProBuilder is Unity's built-in 3D modeling tool that lets designers block out levels directly in the engine without switching to an external DCC tool. Designers who rely on engineers to build blockouts slow iteration to a crawl and lose spatial intuition about how their designs actually feel in 3D.

children.title: Minimum Viable Prototype
children.description: The MVP prototype contains only the single mechanic being validated and nothing else — no menus, no win states, no juice. Adding unrequested features to a prototype obscures the signal you are trying to read and wastes the most valuable asset in design: clarity.

resources.label: Defining the MVP in Game Design

children.title: Playable Build Discipline
children.description: Maintaining a playable build at all times forces incremental integration and surfaces integration bugs early when they are cheap to fix. Projects that only become playable near the end of development face a compression of testing and a flood of costly late bugs.

game-designer.title: Narrative and Branching
game-designer.description: This layer covers story structure and branching narrative using Twine. Narrative design weaves meaning and choice into the player journey.
game-designer.challenge: Create a short branching narrative for your game world in Twine.

children.title: Three-Act Structure in Games
children.description: The three-act arc provides a container for escalating stakes, character growth, and satisfying resolution that players unconsciously expect from any sustained experience. Ignoring structure produces narratives that meander, peak too early, or end anticlimactically, undermining the emotional investment players built across hours of play.

children.title: Branching Narrative Design
children.description: Branching narratives give players the illusion or reality of authorship over the story, but each branch multiplies the content cost; most shipped games use an illusion of choice over true branching. Designers who promise branching without managing combinatorial costs produce either broken promises or unfinished games.

children.title: Dialogue and Character Voice
children.description: Each character should have a distinct voice that signals personality, power, and motivation without requiring explicit exposition. Flat dialogue where all characters sound the same or speak only to deliver information breaks immersion and makes the player feel like they are reading a design document rather than inhabiting a world.

children.title: Worldbuilding for Games
children.description: A game world needs enough internal consistency and lore density that players believe it exists beyond the edges of the screen. Under-built worlds feel like movie sets; over-explained worlds frontload lore that players skip, so designers must balance revelation with mystery.

children.title: Twine for Branching Stories
children.description: Twine makes the structure of branching narratives visual and immediately playable, allowing writers and designers to test story flow without any programming. Writing branching dialogue in a flat document obscures the tree structure and makes it nearly impossible to spot dead ends, loops, or imbalanced branch weights.

children.title: Ink Scripting Language
children.description: Ink by Inkle is a narrative scripting language designed to be embedded in engines, bridging the gap between writer intent and implementation with minimal programmer involvement. Games that implement dialogue entirely in engine code make every narrative change a programmer task, drastically slowing iteration.

children.title: Show Do Not Tell
children.description: Environmental details — a burned photograph, a blocked door, scattered belongings — convey narrative without pausing gameplay, rewarding observant players with a richer understanding of the world. Designers who default to cutscenes or text boxes for every story beat train players to skip dialogue, missing the very story the team worked to tell.

children.title: Lore and Collectibles
children.description: Collectible lore items — journals, audio logs, codex entries — let dedicated players go deeper into the world without forcing that depth on everyone. Mandatory lore dumps break pacing for casual players while optional collectibles satisfy lore-hunters, making the same world feel appropriately deep to both audiences.

game-designer.title: Balancing and Economy
game-designer.description: This layer teaches numerical balancing of stats, costs, and rewards. Balance keeps a game fair, challenging, and satisfying over time.
game-designer.challenge: Build a spreadsheet that balances your game economy and progression.

children.title: Player Onboarding Flows
children.description: Onboarding is the moment when a new player decides whether learning the game is worth the effort; a poor first five minutes loses players who would have loved the game. Flooding new players with controls and systems simultaneously overwhelms working memory and produces abandonment that no amount of later polish can recover.

children.title: Progressive Disclosure
children.description: Progressive disclosure introduces mechanics and interface elements only at the moment the player needs them, keeping cognitive load manageable throughout the experience. Games that reveal the full ruleset in the opening minutes doom players to either ignoring the tutorial or feeling overwhelmed before fun begins.

children.title: HUD Layout and Information Hierarchy
children.description: A HUD must surface the most critical information — health, threat, objective — at maximum visibility while keeping secondary data accessible but non-intrusive. Poor HUD hierarchy causes players to miss critical information mid-action, leading to deaths or failures they attribute to bad game design rather than missed cues.

children.title: Menu and Navigation Design
children.description: Menu architecture must be discoverable, reversible, and consistent — players should never feel trapped in a menu they cannot exit or confused about where a setting lives. Menus that require excessive clicks to reach common options or that obscure destructive actions erode trust and frustrate players before the game even begins.

children.title: Accessibility Options
children.description: Accessibility options — subtitle sizing, colorblind modes, difficulty assists, remappable controls — expand the audience of a game while costing relatively little to implement when planned from the start. Retrofitting accessibility late is expensive; ignoring it entirely locks out a significant portion of potential players and generates negative press.

children.title: Colorblind and Visual Accessibility
children.description: Approximately 8% of men have some form of color vision deficiency, meaning red-green UI elements or health indicators fail for a large fraction of your audience. UI that relies solely on color to communicate state — red for danger, green for safe — is universally considered an accessibility anti-pattern.

children.title: Feedback and Affordances
children.description: Every interactive element must signal its interactability through visual or audio cues — affordances — so players know what they can act upon without trial and error. Objects that look interactive but are not, or interactive objects with no visual signal, produce frustration and break the player's mental model of the game world.

children.title: Wireframing in Figma
children.description: Wireframing UI before building it in-engine lets designers test navigation flows and information hierarchy at near-zero cost. Teams that build UI directly in engine commit to specific layouts before they are validated, making structural changes expensive and discouraging the iteration that produces good UX.

game-designer.title: UX and Interface Design
game-designer.description: This layer covers usability, menus, and clear feedback for players. Good UX removes friction so players engage with the design directly.
game-designer.challenge: Design HUD and menu wireframes for your prototype in Figma.

children.title: Power Curves and Stat Scaling
children.description: Power curves define how player and enemy strength grow over the course of a game; misaligned curves produce skill gaps, trivialized content, or sudden walls that feel arbitrary to players. Designers without explicit curves discover balancing problems only in late-stage playtesting when changing one stat cascades into breaking a dozen systems.

children.title: Currency and Sink Design
children.description: In-game currencies need sinks — meaningful things to spend resources on — that pull currency out of circulation at roughly the same rate it enters. Games without sinks hyperinflate until all resources feel worthless; those with only sinks and no consistent sources make players feel perpetually starved.

children.title: Difficulty Settings and Assists
children.description: Difficulty settings should adjust the challenge to match a player's skill and desired engagement rather than simply changing enemy health numbers. Games with no difficulty options or only numerical scaling miss serving the full spectrum of players from casual to hardcore who could all enjoy the underlying design.

children.title: Tuning Through Data
children.description: Analytics from live players — completion rates, death positions, session length, funnel drop-off — provide ground truth that no amount of internal playtesting can replace. Designers who ignore player data in favor of intuition routinely ship difficulty spikes and friction points that kill retention in the first hours.

children.title: Balancing Spreadsheets
children.description: A balancing spreadsheet models every relevant stat, cost, and reward in one place so a designer can see the global effect of any single change before touching the engine. Working without a spreadsheet means balancing by feel alone, which produces inconsistent results and makes collaborative balancing nearly impossible.

children.title: Simulation and Monte Carlo Testing
children.description: Running Monte Carlo simulations in a spreadsheet — randomizing inputs thousands of times — reveals the distribution of outcomes for systems involving chance and exposes edge cases no single test run will find. Balancing probabilistic systems by hand without simulation produces exploits and frustrations that only emerge after shipping.

children.title: Machinations Economy Diagrams
children.description: Machinations diagrams visualize resource flow in real time and let designers run simulations to spot runaway loops before implementing a single line of code. Economies designed only on paper routinely hit catastrophic imbalances that require costly design-level rewrites after engineering investment.

children.title: Live Operations and Economy Health
children.description: In live games the economy is a living system affected by player behavior that must be monitored and adjusted post-launch with lever systems designed in advance. Designers who do not plan economy tuning levers during production are forced into invasive patches that break player trust when inflation or deflation emerges.

game-designer.title: Playtesting and Iteration
game-designer.description: This layer focuses on running playtests and iterating from feedback. Disciplined testing exposes problems no amount of theory can predict.
game-designer.challenge: Run a structured playtest of your prototype and document changes to make.

children.title: Structuring a Playtest Session
children.description: A structured playtest defines specific hypotheses to validate, recruits the target player profile, and standardizes observation conditions so results are comparable across sessions. Unstructured playtesting generates vague impressions rather than actionable data, and designers end up changing things based on noise rather than signal.

children.title: Observing Without Interfering
children.description: The most valuable playtest data comes from watching players silently — noting where they stop, hesitate, or fail — rather than correcting them or answering their questions. Designers who guide players through confusion during a test are validating their own explanations, not the design itself.

children.title: Synthesizing Feedback
children.description: Raw playtest feedback is full of contradictions, misattributions, and symptom descriptions rather than root causes; synthesizing it means identifying patterns and translating them into design hypotheses. Acting on the loudest single voice rather than patterns from multiple sessions produces erratic design that satisfies no one.

children.title: Avoiding Designer Bias
children.description: Designer bias causes creators to rationalize poor player experiences as user error rather than design failure, dismissing valid feedback that contradicts their vision. A designer who cannot separate love for their creation from objective assessment of its failures will never meaningfully improve a game through iteration.

children.title: Playtest Surveys and Metrics
children.description: Post-session surveys using standardized scales — like the Game Experience Questionnaire — provide quantifiable emotion and engagement data that complements observed behavior. Open-ended qualitative feedback alone is hard to track across sessions; metrics let you see whether changes between builds actually moved the needles you intended.

children.title: Analytics Integration
children.description: In-game telemetry — event logging, heatmaps, funnel analysis — scales playtest insights to real player populations and reveals patterns invisible in small session sizes. Designers who rely solely on hand-picked playtesters ship games with difficulty spikes and UX confusion that only appear at scale.

children.title: The Iteration Loop
children.description: The design iteration loop — hypothesize, prototype, test, analyze, repeat — is the fundamental process that separates professional design from guesswork. Designers who skip steps or rush iterations produce changes that feel arbitrary to the team and fail to improve the experience measurably.

children.title: Knowing When to Pivot
children.description: A playtest that consistently shows a core mechanic is not fun is evidence that the design needs a fundamental change, not more polish. Designers who cannot distinguish solvable surface problems from broken foundations waste production time polishing experiences that will never be enjoyable.

game-designer.title: Pitching and Shipping
game-designer.description: This layer covers pitching, vertical slices, and preparing a design for release. Communicating and finalizing the vision is what gets a game made and shipped.
game-designer.challenge: Produce a polished pitch deck and vertical slice plan for your game.

children.title: Premium vs Free-to-Play
children.description: The choice between premium and free-to-play fundamentally changes every design decision from progression pacing to content gating and session length. Designers who adopt a monetization model without understanding how it reshapes the player relationship produce games that feel either exploitative or underfunded.

children.title: Battle Passes and Season Models
children.description: Battle passes trade a flat fee for time-limited content that drives regular engagement and provides predictable recurring revenue. Poorly designed passes create FOMO anxiety that burns players out; well-designed passes provide achievable goals that reward consistent but not excessive play.

children.title: Pay-to-Win vs Cosmetic Monetization
children.description: Pay-to-win monetization that sells competitive advantage fundamentally alters the social contract of a multiplayer game, converting skill competition into wealth competition. Players perceive this as a betrayal of fairness and it causes long-term reputational and retention damage that purely cosmetic monetization avoids.

children.title: Loot Box and Gacha Design
children.description: Gacha and loot box mechanics leverage variable reward schedules — the most powerful psychological reinforcement mechanism — to drive spending, but they also attract regulatory scrutiny and player backlash when poorly implemented. Designers using these mechanics bear a responsibility to set fair disclosure, spending caps, and pity systems.

resources.label: Gacha and Loot Box Design Ethics

children.title: Daily Engagement Loops
children.description: Daily engagement loops — login bonuses, daily quests, limited-time challenges — provide short-term goals that keep players returning without requiring major content releases. Poorly designed daily loops become chores that feel obligatory rather than rewarding, creating guilt-driven retention that predicts churn rather than satisfaction.

children.title: Conversion Funnel Design
children.description: The conversion funnel maps the player journey from first session through first purchase, identifying the moments where players decide the game is worth paying for. Designers who ignore conversion invest in monetization systems that are never seen because players churn before reaching them.

children.title: Transparency and Fairness
children.description: Players who understand how a monetization system works and feel it is fair are significantly more likely to spend and to recommend the game. Opaque pricing, hidden odds, and manipulative dark patterns generate short-term revenue at the cost of long-term community health and brand reputation.

children.title: Dark Patterns to Avoid
children.description: Dark patterns — artificial scarcity, misleading countdown timers, buried unsubscribe flows — exploit cognitive biases to extract money from players without providing genuine value. Regulators globally are increasingly legislating against these practices, making avoidance both an ethical imperative and a legal risk management strategy.

godot.title: Godot and GDScript Basics
godot.description: This layer introduces the Godot editor and the GDScript language. These fundamentals let you build and script nodes for any game.
godot.challenge: Build a scene with a sprite node that moves using keyboard input.

children.title: Typed vs Dynamic Variables
children.description: GDScript allows both dynamic and statically typed variables. Skipping type hints makes refactoring error-prone and hides bugs until runtime, whereas typed code gives autocompletion and catches mismatches early.

children.title: Lifecycle Methods: _ready and _process
children.description: _ready runs once when the node enters the scene tree; _process runs every frame. Confusing them causes initialization code to fire repeatedly or one-time setup to be missed entirely, breaking spawning and state logic.

children.title: Scene Tree and Node Inspector
children.description: The scene tree shows hierarchy at a glance; the inspector exposes every exported property. Misreading the tree leads to accidental parent-child coupling, while ignoring the inspector means tuning values through hard-coded scripts instead of design-friendly fields.

children.title: Debugger and Output Panel
children.description: The built-in debugger shows call stacks, variable state, and error traces. Ignoring it means guessing at runtime bugs rather than pinpointing the exact script line and variable value causing the problem.

children.title: Built-in Signals
children.description: Godot nodes expose built-in signals for events like button presses and body collisions. Missing them forces polling in _process, wasting CPU and introducing frame-lag in event responses.

children.title: Connecting Signals in Code vs Editor
children.description: Signals can be wired in the editor or in code via connect(). Editor connections are fragile when scenes are refactored; code connections break when node paths change. Knowing both approaches lets you pick the right tool for each situation.

children.title: Running a Single Scene
children.description: Godot lets you run any scene independently with F6. Skipping this shortcut wastes time relaunching the full project to test a single isolated mechanic or UI screen.

children.title: Export Variables for Rapid Tuning
children.description: @export exposes script variables to the inspector so designers can tweak values without touching code. Without it, every balance change requires a code edit, rebuild, and re-run cycle that slows iteration dramatically.

godot.title: Nodes, Scenes, and Signals
godot.description: This layer explores composing reusable scenes and connecting them with signals. The node and signal model is the heart of Godot architecture.
godot.challenge: Create reusable enemy and coin scenes that emit signals when collected.

children.title: Scene Instancing and Reuse
children.description: Every scene in Godot can be instanced as a node inside another scene. Not instancing scenes leads to copy-pasted node trees that must be updated in dozens of places when a single enemy or pickup needs a change.

children.title: Scene Inheritance
children.description: Inherited scenes let a child scene override parts of a base scene without duplicating the full tree. Ignoring inheritance means identical base behavior gets pasted into every variant, making the project fragile and hard to maintain.

children.title: Defining and Emitting Custom Signals
children.description: Custom signals decouple nodes so a coin does not need a direct reference to the score manager. Without them, you end up with tangled get_node paths that break whenever the scene tree is reorganized.

children.title: Passing Data Through Signals
children.description: Signals can carry arguments so a health node emits current_health to any interested listener. Omitting arguments forces listeners to query the emitter directly, re-introducing the coupling signals were meant to eliminate.

children.title: Autoload Singletons
children.description: Autoloads are nodes loaded before every scene and accessible globally by name. Without them, shared state like a score or settings object must be passed manually between scenes, causing complex and error-prone chains of node references.

children.title: Avoiding Autoload Overuse
children.description: Autoloads should hold globally relevant state, not game logic that belongs in individual scenes. Stuffing too much into a singleton creates hidden dependencies that make scenes impossible to test in isolation.

children.title: Using Groups for Broadcast Calls
children.description: Groups let one node call a method on every node in a group with a single line. Without groups, broadcasting a damage event to all enemies requires storing and iterating a manual array, adding bookkeeping that breaks when enemies are freed mid-frame.

children.title: Groups vs Signals for Events
children.description: Groups are best for broadcasting to an unknown set of receivers; signals are better when the sender knows its audience. Mixing them up leads to either over-connected signal webs or broadcast calls silently failing because no group member was registered.

godot.title: 2D Physics and Movement
godot.description: This layer covers the 2D physics bodies and building a character controller. Reliable movement and collision drive a satisfying platformer.
godot.challenge: Build a platformer character with gravity, jumping, and coin pickups.

children.title: CharacterBody2D vs RigidBody2D
children.description: CharacterBody2D gives you full manual control via move_and_slide; RigidBody2D hands movement to the physics engine. Choosing the wrong body type causes either jittery physics-driven characters or sluggish manually-controlled objects that cannot respond to forces.

children.title: Collision Shapes and Layers
children.description: Each body needs a CollisionShape2D and the right collision layer and mask bits to determine what it interacts with. Wrong layer assignments cause bullets to collide with allies or players to clip through walls with no obvious error.

children.title: move_and_slide and Floor Detection
children.description: move_and_slide handles slope sliding and floor detection in one call. Manually moving a CharacterBody2D with position += velocity misses collision resolution, causing the character to walk through walls and not register as on the floor.

children.title: Delta Time and Frame-Rate Independence
children.description: Multiplying velocity by delta in _physics_process ensures movement speed is the same on 30 FPS and 144 FPS machines. Omitting delta makes your game run twice as fast on a high-refresh monitor and unplayably slow on a weak device.

children.title: Area2D for Triggers and Pickups
children.description: Area2D detects when bodies enter or exit a region without blocking movement. Using a StaticBody for a pickup area instead blocks the player, while using no body at all means the pickup can never be collected.

children.title: body_entered vs area_entered Signals
children.description: body_entered fires when a physics body enters the area; area_entered fires when another Area2D overlaps it. Connecting the wrong signal means hitboxes never register or fire on the wrong object type, silently breaking combat and pickup detection.

children.title: Implementing Gravity Manually
children.description: In Godot 4 you must add gravity yourself each physics frame rather than relying on automatic body gravity for CharacterBody2D. Forgetting this leaves your character floating, while applying it outside _physics_process causes stuttering tied to frame rate.

children.title: Jump Buffering and Coyote Time
children.description: Jump buffering queues an input slightly before landing; coyote time extends the jump window past a platform edge. Omitting both makes jumps feel unresponsive and causes players to miss platforms repeatedly, blaming the game rather than their timing.

godot.title: UI and Game Flow
godot.description: This layer teaches building menus and HUDs using Control nodes. UI and scene switching turn mechanics into a full game loop.
godot.challenge: Add a main menu, score HUD, and game over screen to the platformer.

children.title: Anchors and Size Flags
children.description: Anchors define how a Control node repositions when its parent resizes; size flags control stretch behavior inside containers. Hardcoding pixel positions without anchors breaks your UI on every screen size different from your editor preview.

children.title: Container Nodes for Responsive UI
children.description: VBoxContainer, HBoxContainer, and GridContainer automatically arrange children and reflow on resize. Manually positioning child nodes in a container defeats the layout system and causes elements to overlap when text length or window size changes.

children.title: Updating Labels and ProgressBars via Signals
children.description: Health and score UI should update reactively through signals rather than polling state in _process. Polling introduces one-frame lag and burns CPU re-setting values that have not changed, while signal-driven updates are instant and free when nothing changes.

children.title: Theme Resources for Consistent Styling
children.description: A Theme resource centralizes fonts, colors, and style boxes so every button and label shares the same look. Without a theme, styling each Control node individually means a color change requires editing every node in the project one by one.

children.title: SceneTree.change_scene_to_file
children.description: change_scene_to_file replaces the current scene cleanly and frees its memory. Using remove_child and add_child manually to switch scenes leaks the old scene tree in memory and requires careful cleanup that is easy to get wrong.

children.title: Transition Animations Between Scenes
children.description: A ColorRect overlay animated with a tween creates smooth fade transitions between scenes. Without transitions, instant scene swaps feel jarring and cheap, especially on slower hardware where the loading pause is visible.

children.title: SceneTree.paused and Process Modes
children.description: Setting get_tree().paused = true stops all nodes except those with process_mode set to Always. Implementing pause by manually stopping every node individually misses edge cases and causes inconsistent behavior when new nodes are added later.

children.title: Restart and Reload Logic
children.description: Reloading the current scene with reload_current_scene resets all state cleanly in one call. Manually resetting variables risks missing a field and starting the next run with leftover state from the previous session, causing mysterious bugs.

godot.title: Animation and Tweens
godot.description: This layer covers AnimationPlayer, AnimatedSprite, and tweening for motion. Animation adds polish and clear feedback to gameplay.
godot.challenge: Animate the character with idle run and jump states and tweened pickups.

children.title: Keyframe Tracks and Interpolation
children.description: AnimationPlayer animates any node property through keyframe tracks with selectable interpolation modes. Without understanding tracks, animators resort to moving objects in code each frame, making animations impossible to preview or tweak in the editor.

children.title: Animation Callbacks and Method Tracks
children.description: Method tracks let an animation call a GDScript function at a precise keyframe, such as spawning a projectile at the peak of an attack swing. Without them, timing gameplay events to animations requires fragile frame-counting hacks in _process.

children.title: AnimationTree with StateMachine Playback
children.description: AnimationTree drives complex character animation graphs through state machines and blend nodes. Without it, switching between idle, run, and attack by calling play() directly produces abrupt cuts and makes transition conditions scattered across multiple scripts.

children.title: Blend Positions for Directional Movement
children.description: BlendSpace2D nodes blend animations by a 2D axis so the character animates correctly when moving in any direction. Hardcoding separate animations for each cardinal direction causes eight-way movement to look robotic and misses diagonal blending entirely.

children.title: SpriteFrames Resource and Frame Rate
children.description: AnimatedSprite2D plays frame sequences defined in a SpriteFrames resource at a configurable frame rate. Setting the rate too high makes animations play at different speeds depending on game FPS when not using the built-in timer, causing visual jitter.

children.title: animation_finished Signal
children.description: animation_finished fires when a non-looping animation ends, allowing transitions like returning to idle after an attack. Polling is_playing() instead misses the exact finish frame and introduces one-frame state delays that break combo timing.

children.title: Tween Property and Method Calls
children.description: Tweens interpolate any property over time with easing curves, ideal for UI pop-ins and item bounces. Animating these effects in _process with manual lerp math is more verbose, harder to cancel cleanly, and does not compose well when multiple tweens run simultaneously.

children.title: Chaining Tweens and await
children.description: Tween steps can be chained sequentially and awaited with await tween.finished in a coroutine. Without chaining, orchestrating a sequence of movements requires nesting signals or using timers, which scatters multi-step animation logic across multiple callbacks.

godot.title: Audio and Particles
godot.description: This layer adds sound, music, and particle effects for immersion. Audio and effects make interactions feel responsive and alive.
godot.challenge: Add jump and pickup sounds, music, and a particle burst to the game.

children.title: AudioStreamPlayer vs AudioStreamPlayer2D
children.description: AudioStreamPlayer plays audio globally; AudioStreamPlayer2D attenuates volume based on distance from the listener. Using the global player for footsteps and environment sounds destroys immersion because all sounds play at full volume regardless of their source position.

children.title: Playing Sounds Without Overlapping
children.description: Calling play() on a node that is already playing restarts it from the beginning. Using multiple AudioStreamPlayers in an audio pool or checking is_playing() prevents sound effects from cutting each other off during rapid repeated actions like fast gunfire.

children.title: Audio Bus Layout and Effects
children.description: The Audio Bus Layout assigns streams to named buses where you can apply reverb, compression, and EQ. Without buses, applying a reverb effect to all room sounds requires adding an effect to every individual AudioStreamPlayer in the scene.

children.title: Volume Control with Bus Linear Volume
children.description: Master, Music, and SFX buses let players control each category independently. Storing volume as a raw linear value and applying it to bus_set_volume_db after conversion prevents the logarithmic mismatch that makes a slider at 50% sound almost silent.

children.title: Particle Process Material Settings
children.description: GPUParticles2D drives particle behavior through a ParticleProcessMaterial that controls velocity, gravity, scale, and color over lifetime. Animating particles in GDScript instead of the material bypasses GPU acceleration and tanks performance during heavy effect moments.

children.title: One-Shot Bursts vs Continuous Emitters
children.description: Setting one_shot = true and emitting programmatically creates a burst that auto-frees; looping emitters are for persistent effects like rain or fire. Using a looping emitter for a pickup burst leaves orphaned particles running after the pickup is gone.

children.title: Freeing Effect Nodes After Playback
children.description: One-shot particle nodes and short AudioStreamPlayers should call queue_free() after finishing. Spawning effects without freeing them fills the scene tree with invisible spent nodes that accumulate over a session and steadily increase memory and draw calls.

children.title: Synchronizing Audio and Visual Feedback
children.description: Triggering a sound and a particle burst in the same frame from the same signal handler keeps audio and visual feedback perfectly synchronized. Triggering them from separate code paths introduces millisecond offsets that players notice as a disconnect between seeing and hearing an event.

godot.title: Shaders in Godot
godot.description: This layer introduces the Godot shading language for custom 2D effects. Shaders let you craft a distinctive visual identity.
godot.challenge: Write a hit flash and outline shader for enemies in the game.

children.title: Shader Types: canvas_item vs spatial
children.description: canvas_item shaders target 2D nodes; spatial shaders target 3D MeshInstance nodes. Applying a spatial shader to a Sprite2D or vice versa produces a blank material because the vertex and fragment inputs differ fundamentally between the two pipelines.

children.title: Uniforms for Designer-Tweakable Parameters
children.description: Uniform variables expose shader parameters to the inspector so artists can adjust color, speed, or intensity without editing code. Hard-coding these values means every visual tweak requires a shader edit, breaking material instances that share the same base shader.

children.title: Outline and Hit Flash Shaders
children.description: Outline shaders sample neighboring pixels to detect edges; hit flash shaders replace the albedo with a solid color for one frame. Without them, communicating damage requires spawning separate overlay sprites, doubling draw calls and complicating z-order management.

children.title: Dissolve Shaders with Noise Textures
children.description: Dissolve shaders use a noise texture and a threshold uniform to progressively discard fragments, creating burn or disintegration effects. Attempting this with sprite frame sequences requires an artist to pre-render every dissolve frame, multiplying asset size dramatically.

children.title: Minimizing Texture Samples per Fragment
children.description: Each texture lookup in a fragment shader costs GPU bandwidth and can become a bottleneck on mobile. Combining data into channel-packed textures reduces sample counts, and understanding this tradeoff prevents accidental performance cliffs on low-end devices.

children.title: Shader Variants and Conditionals
children.description: Using if statements in shaders runs both branches on many GPU architectures due to SIMD execution. Replacing conditionals with step() and mix() calls produces branchless shaders that execute faster and more predictably across desktop and mobile GPUs.

children.title: Node-Based Shader Authoring
children.description: The VisualShader editor provides a node graph alternative to writing shader code directly. Knowing when to switch from visual to code mode prevents hitting graph complexity limits on advanced effects while still giving artists an approachable entry point.

children.title: Converting Visual Shaders to Code
children.description: Godot can copy the generated GLSL from a VisualShader, letting you start visually and refine in code for features the graph cannot express. Staying locked in the visual editor blocks access to loops and custom functions that advanced effects require.

godot.title: C# in Godot and Architecture
godot.description: This layer adds C# scripting and clean project architecture in Godot. C# brings strong typing and tooling for larger codebases.
godot.challenge: Rewrite a core system in C# and add a save and load feature.

children.title: C# Setup and .NET SDK Requirements
children.description: Godot's C# support requires the .NET SDK and the Mono-enabled editor build. Missing either gives cryptic build errors that look like engine bugs; knowing the setup requirements prevents hours of troubleshooting a misconfigured environment.

children.title: Calling Godot APIs from C#
children.description: Godot's C# bindings use PascalCase and nullable reference types that differ from GDScript's snake_case API. Copying GDScript examples directly into C# produces compile errors; understanding the naming convention converts any docs example in seconds.

children.title: Custom Resource Classes
children.description: Extending Resource creates data containers editable in the inspector and saveable as .tres files. Storing game data in Dictionary or plain GDScript variables loses inspector integration and requires custom serialization code that duplicates what Resources provide for free.

children.title: Preloading vs Loading Resources
children.description: preload() loads a resource at parse time and guarantees it is ready instantly; load() loads it at runtime and can return null if the path is wrong. Using load() for assets that must exist at startup leads to null reference crashes that only appear after a cold start.

children.title: Saving with FileAccess and JSON
children.description: FileAccess writes text or binary files; JSON serializes node state into a portable format. Relying on ProjectSettings or global variables for save state loses all progress when the app is closed because neither persists to disk automatically.

children.title: Versioning Save Files
children.description: Adding a version field to save data lets you migrate old saves when the schema changes in an update. Ignoring versioning means any game update that adds or renames a field corrupts existing player saves and forces a full wipe.

children.title: Folder and File Naming Conventions
children.description: A consistent folder structure separating scenes, scripts, assets, and resources by feature prevents the project root from becoming a flat pile of hundreds of files. Without structure, finding and refactoring files slows down significantly as the project grows.

children.title: Decoupling Scripts with Interfaces
children.description: GDScript duck-typing and C# interfaces let scripts interact through contracts rather than concrete types. Tight coupling to node paths breaks every time the scene tree is reorganized, while interface-based design survives structural changes without cascading fixes.

godot.title: Enemy AI and Levels
godot.description: This layer builds enemy AI with navigation and assembles full levels. Reactive enemies and varied levels make a complete experience.
godot.challenge: Add patrolling and chasing enemies and build a multi-level world.

children.title: State-Based AI with Enums
children.description: Using an enum and a match statement per _physics_process tick implements a clean state machine for patrol, chase, and attack. Nesting if/else chains for state handling grows unmanageable past three states and makes adding new behaviors error-prone.

children.title: Player Detection with RayCast2D
children.description: A RayCast2D from the enemy toward the player tests line of sight, preventing detection through walls. Using only distance checks means enemies spot the player through solid geometry and teleport to attack positions that should be blocked.

children.title: NavigationRegion2D and NavigationAgent2D
children.description: NavigationRegion2D bakes a walkable polygon; NavigationAgent2D queries it for a path to a target. Hand-coding pathfinding with grid arrays reinvents a solved problem and cannot handle the organic shapes that tile-based navigation meshes support out of the box.

children.title: Navigation Layers for Enemy Types
children.description: Navigation layers let different enemy types traverse different regions, such as flying enemies ignoring ground obstacles. Sharing a single navigation map for all agent types forces every agent to avoid every obstacle, producing unrealistic movement for non-ground units.

children.title: TileSet Physics Layers and Terrain Sets
children.description: TileSet physics layers assign collision shapes to tiles; terrain sets auto-tile adjacent tiles. Building levels with individual Sprite2D nodes instead of TileMaps multiplies the node count dramatically and loses the auto-tiling and physics baking that TileMaps provide.

children.title: Procedural TileMap Generation
children.description: TileMaps can be filled programmatically using set_cell(), enabling procedural level generation at runtime. Without this knowledge, procedural dungeons require instantiating hundreds of individual tile nodes, overwhelming the renderer with draw calls.

children.title: Object Pooling for Enemy Spawning
children.description: Pre-instantiating enemies into a pool and reusing freed nodes avoids the garbage collection spikes that occur when spawning and freeing dozens of enemies per second. Ignoring pooling causes visible frame drops during wave-based encounters on low-end hardware.

children.title: Passing Data Between Levels
children.description: Autoload singletons persist across scene changes, making them the right place to store which level is next and player carry-over stats. Trying to pass data through node references breaks on scene reload because the old nodes are freed before the new scene reads them.

godot.title: Exporting and Optimization
godot.description: This layer covers profiling, optimizing, and exporting to platforms. Producing a shippable build is the final step to release.
godot.challenge: Profile and optimize the game then export a build for desktop and web.

children.title: Reading the Profiler Timeline
children.description: The Godot profiler shows time spent in each script function per frame on a timeline. Guessing at bottlenecks without the profiler leads to optimizing code that was never slow while missing the actual culprit, often a single poorly-written _process loop.

children.title: Reducing _process Call Overhead
children.description: Every node with a _process override runs every frame even if it has nothing to do. Disabling set_process(false) when a node is idle, or restructuring to use signals and timers instead, can eliminate dozens of needless script calls per frame in scene-heavy levels.

children.title: CanvasItem Batching
children.description: Godot batches 2D draw calls automatically when items share the same texture and material. Mixing textures frequently or adding unique materials to every sprite breaks batching and multiplies draw calls, causing GPU overhead that tanks frame rate on mobile.

children.title: Texture Atlases for Sprite Batching
children.description: Packing sprites into a single atlas texture ensures they share one draw call batch. Using hundreds of individual PNG files forces the GPU to rebind textures constantly, which is one of the most common and avoidable performance mistakes in 2D Godot projects.

children.title: Installing Export Templates
children.description: Godot requires platform-specific export templates downloaded separately from the editor. Attempting to export without them produces a hard error, and using mismatched template versions causes crashes in the exported build that do not appear in the editor.

children.title: Export Presets and Feature Tags
children.description: Export presets configure icons, splash screens, and feature flags per platform. Ignoring feature tags means debug-only code and test assets ship in the release build, increasing file size and potentially exposing internal tooling to end users.

children.title: Web Export and COOP Headers
children.description: Godot's HTML5 export requires Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy headers on the server for SharedArrayBuffer and threads to work. Hosting on a server without these headers causes the web build to crash immediately or run single-threaded at reduced performance.

children.title: itch.io Project Page Setup
children.description: Setting the correct viewport dimensions and enabling the itch.io HTML embed mode ensures the game fits the page without scroll bars. Wrong dimensions clip the game window and break mouse input coordinates, making the published build unplayable even though it ran fine locally.

game-ai.title: AI Foundations
game-ai.description: This layer introduces game AI concepts and the math behind agent behavior. A grounding in fundamentals prepares you for advanced techniques.
game-ai.challenge: Implement a simple agent that moves toward a target each frame.

children.title: Agents and Autonomy
children.description: An agent is any entity that perceives its environment and acts to achieve goals. Misunderstanding agent autonomy leads to AI that feels scripted and predictable rather than reactive and believable.

resources.label: Red Blob Games — Introduction to AI

children.title: Determinism and Randomness
children.description: Balancing deterministic and stochastic behavior controls AI repeatability and fairness. Over-reliance on randomness creates jittery AI; ignoring it makes enemies exploitable and monotonous.

children.title: Vectors and Dot Products
children.description: Vectors underpin every movement and sensing calculation in game AI. Misusing dot products for angle checks or direction comparisons produces incorrect detection cones and broken pursuit logic.

resources.label: Freya Holmer — Math for Game Devs

children.title: Update Loops and Time Budgets
children.description: AI update frequency must be decoupled from render frames to stay stable and performant. Running AI every frame on hundreds of agents tanks performance; missing time-slice patterns causes hitchy gameplay.

children.title: Start with Simple Seek
children.description: Implementing a basic seek behavior first gives you a testable agent loop before any decision architecture is added. Skipping this step means debugging complex systems without a verified movement baseline.

children.title: Reading AI for Games by Millington
children.description: Ian Millington's book is the definitive reference covering every major technique from movement to learning. Practitioners who skip it routinely reinvent inferior versions of well-understood algorithms.

children.title: Debug Visualization
children.description: Drawing sight cones, paths, and state labels in the viewport is essential for understanding what AI is actually doing. Without it, you are debugging blindly and will miss subtle but game-breaking edge cases.

children.title: Profiling AI Cost
children.description: AI logic is often the hidden CPU bottleneck in large scenes because it touches physics, pathfinding, and decision trees every tick. Profiling before optimizing prevents wasted effort on the wrong systems.

resources.label: GDC Vault — CPU Optimization for AI

game-ai.title: Finite State Machines
game-ai.description: This layer covers finite state machines for structuring agent behavior. FSMs are the most common and approachable AI architecture.
game-ai.challenge: Build an enemy with idle patrol and chase states using an FSM.

children.title: States and Transitions
children.description: A state machine is only as good as its transition guards; badly defined conditions cause states to thrash rapidly or never exit. Clearly specifying entry, update, and exit methods for every state prevents brittle enemy behavior.

children.title: Hierarchical State Machines
children.description: Hierarchical FSMs allow states to inherit shared behavior, drastically reducing code duplication in complex AI. Ignoring HFSM leads to an explosion of nearly identical states that become unmaintainable as the game grows.

children.title: Enter, Update, Exit Callbacks
children.description: Separating lifecycle callbacks ensures side effects like animations and sounds fire exactly once and clean up properly on exit. Missing exit callbacks commonly causes stuck animation states and ghost sounds.

children.title: Data-Driven Transition Tables
children.description: Externalizing transition rules into data lets designers tune AI without touching code. Hard-coding transitions in switch statements makes iterating enemy behavior slow and error-prone.

children.title: State Thrashing
children.description: Rapidly toggling between states because the transition condition oscillates produces jittery, unrealistic behavior and wastes CPU. Adding hysteresis or minimum dwell times eliminates this class of bug entirely.

resources.label: Game AI Pro — Avoiding State Thrash

children.title: God States
children.description: Cramming too much logic into a single state defeats the purpose of an FSM and makes debugging nearly impossible. Splitting large states into sub-machines restores clarity and localizability.

children.title: Unity Animator vs Custom FSM
children.description: Unity's Animator is optimized for blended animation states, not arbitrary logic; mixing game-logic conditions into it creates fragile spaghetti. Use a separate code-side FSM for AI decisions and drive the Animator as a side effect.

children.title: Unreal Behavior Tree Integration
children.description: Unreal's built-in Behavior Tree system provides blackboard-backed FSM-like states with editor tooling. Understanding how tasks map to states prevents duplication of work when graduating from FSM to BT.

game-ai.title: Pathfinding and Navigation
game-ai.description: This layer teaches grid and navmesh pathfinding with the A star algorithm. Pathfinding lets agents move intelligently through the world.
game-ai.challenge: Implement A star pathfinding so enemies navigate around obstacles to the player.

children.title: Open and Closed Lists
children.description: A* relies on correctly maintaining open and closed sets to guarantee the shortest path without revisiting nodes. Errors in list management produce infinite loops, missed paths, or catastrophically slow searches.

children.title: Heuristics and Admissibility
children.description: A heuristic must never overestimate the true cost to guarantee A* finds the optimal path. A non-admissible heuristic speeds up search but silently returns suboptimal routes that make AI look stupid.

children.title: NavMesh Baking and Agents
children.description: A NavMesh encodes walkable surfaces as a polygon network allowing agents to plan paths without a dense grid. Incorrect bake settings cause agents to clip geometry, fall off ledges, or refuse to reach valid destinations.

children.title: Path Smoothing and Funneling
children.description: Raw NavMesh paths are jagged polygon-center chains; the funnel algorithm converts them to smooth straight-line segments. Skipping smoothing causes agents to walk in unnatural zigzag patterns that break immersion.

children.title: Dynamic Obstacle Avoidance
children.description: Static pathfinding breaks when obstacles move; techniques like RVO allow agents to negotiate shared space in real time. Ignoring dynamic obstacles produces agents that tunnel through moving objects or freeze completely.

children.title: Hierarchical Pathfinding
children.description: Large open worlds require hierarchical pathfinding that plans at macro then micro levels to stay within frame budgets. Flat A* on massive graphs stalls the game loop; HPA* and similar methods cut search cost by orders of magnitude.

children.title: Grid vs Graph Representation
children.description: Choosing the wrong spatial representation for your game type inflates memory and search time unnecessarily. Grids suit tile-based games while navigation meshes suit 3D environments; mixing them requires careful abstraction.

children.title: Off-Mesh Links
children.description: Off-mesh links let agents traverse jumps, ladders, and teleporters that the NavMesh cannot represent geometrically. Forgetting to add them means enemies cannot follow the player through the most interesting parts of a level.

game-ai.title: Steering Behaviors
game-ai.description: This layer covers steering behaviors like seek, flee, and flocking. Steering produces smooth lifelike movement that pathfinding alone lacks.
game-ai.challenge: Add flocking and obstacle avoidance to a group of patrolling enemies.

children.title: Seek, Flee, and Arrive
children.description: Seek steers toward a target at full speed; arrive adds a deceleration radius so the agent doesn't overshoot. Using seek alone for all movement causes comical overshooting and jitter that breaks any illusion of intelligence.

children.title: Pursue and Evade
children.description: Pursue predicts the target's future position rather than chasing where they are now, producing smarter interception. Agents that seek the current position instead of the predicted one always lag behind fast-moving players.

children.title: Flocking: Separation, Alignment, Cohesion
children.description: Reynolds' three rules produce emergent crowd movement from simple per-agent calculations. Tuning the three weights incorrectly causes flocks to implode, scatter indefinitely, or all move in a single rigid block.

resources.label: Craig Reynolds — Flocks, Herds, and Schools

children.title: Obstacle Avoidance
children.description: Obstacle avoidance steers around geometry that pathfinding did not account for at a local level. Without it, agents that successfully reach a navmesh waypoint still walk straight into dynamic furniture or other agents.

resources.label: Game Developer — Local Avoidance Deep Dive

children.title: Weighted Blending vs Prioritized
children.description: Weighted blending sums all steering forces scaled by priority while prioritized truncation applies behaviors in order until the force budget is spent. Choosing the wrong combiner creates agents that oscillate or ignore critical avoidance impulses.

children.title: Context Steering
children.description: Context steering maps interest and danger into directional slots for a cleaner composable system than raw force summation. It avoids the common problem of opposing forces canceling each other and leaving the agent frozen.

children.title: Steering in Unity with NavMeshAgent
children.description: Unity's NavMeshAgent provides built-in steering and avoidance but its internal force model conflicts with manual rigidbody manipulation. Understanding which properties to override prevents agents from rubber-banding or ignoring custom forces.

children.title: Steering in Unreal with Move Components
children.description: Unreal's AIMoveTo and the CharacterMovementComponent handle steering under the hood; customizing it requires overriding the movement component. Missing this separation leads to fighting between native movement and custom steering impulses.

game-ai.title: Behavior Trees
game-ai.description: This layer introduces behavior trees as a scalable decision architecture. Behavior trees handle complex AI more cleanly than large state machines.

resources.label: Behavior Trees in Robotics and AI

game-ai.challenge: Replace the enemy FSM with a behavior tree controlling patrol and combat.

children.title: Selectors and Sequences
children.description: A selector tries children until one succeeds while a sequence runs children until one fails; these two nodes form the entire logic backbone of a behavior tree. Confusing their semantics inverts your AI logic silently and is the most common BT bug.

children.title: Decorator and Service Nodes
children.description: Decorators modify a child's result or run frequency while services update blackboard values on a timer for their subtree. Overloading leaf tasks with side effects instead of using services couples decision logic to execution logic unnecessarily.

resources.label: Unreal Docs — Behavior Tree Node Types
GDC Vault — Behavior Tree Design Patterns

children.title: Shared Blackboard State
children.description: A blackboard is a typed key-value store that decouples perception, planning, and execution nodes from each other. Without it, tasks pass data through brittle global variables or hard-wired component references that break on refactor.

children.title: Blackboard Key Types and Invalidation
children.description: Correctly typing blackboard keys prevents silent type mismatches and stale values that cause tasks to act on outdated world knowledge. Forgetting to clear a last-known-position key after the enemy loses sight leads to X-ray-chasing exploits.

children.title: Reactive vs Memory-Based Trees
children.description: Reactive trees re-evaluate conditions every tick for immediate responsiveness; memory trees only check when a subtree finishes, preserving commitment to a task. Using the wrong mode makes enemies either hyper-jittery or dangerously slow to react.

children.title: Parallel Nodes
children.description: Parallel nodes run multiple children simultaneously, enabling an AI to patrol and scan for threats at the same time. Misunderstanding parallel policy settings (succeed-on-all vs succeed-on-one) inverts combined behavior in hard-to-trace ways.

children.title: Unreal Behavior Tree Editor
